proposals.title: StreamBench: Real-Time Benchmarking of LLMs via Continuous Ingestion of Live Chat Data
proposals.motivation: Static benchmarks fail to capture the dynamic, evolving nature of language use and user intent in real-world applications like chatbots and social media. This creates a growing gap between reported model performance and actual user experience. A system that continuously updates evaluation tasks based on live data is needed to assess models under realistic, temporally shifting conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that continuously ingests live chat data to dynamically generate and update benchmark prompts can provide a more accurate, timely, and cost-effective measure of LLM performance on real-world tasks compared to static benchmarks.
proposals.method: We propose a streaming architecture built on Ray or similar distributed systems, inspired by Ion Stoica's work on efficient scheduling and resource management. The system will ingest public chat streams (e.g., from Reddit, Twitter API), use lightweight filtering and clustering (e.g., BARE-inspired few-shot prompting) to identify novel query patterns and intents, and automatically synthesize new evaluation prompts. A scheduler, extending concepts from Continuum, will manage the cost and latency of running these evolving benchmarks against target models, potentially using KV cache optimizations and sleep-time compute strategies for efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for real-time LLM evaluation, moving beyond static snapshots. It will provide a practical system for tracking model performance drift relative to live user behavior and establish metrics for benchmark freshness and coverage of emerging topics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on optimizing inference for static queries, this system treats the benchmark itself as a dynamic, streaming resource that must be managed and updated efficiently, applying resource-aware scheduling to the evaluation process.

proposals.title: Targeted Data Synthesis via Inference-Time Weakness Detection for Efficient LLM Improvement
proposals.motivation: Current methods for improving LLMs rely on large, static, and often redundant datasets, which is computationally expensive and inefficient for addressing specific model deficiencies. As models scale, identifying and correcting systematic weaknesses—such as failures in temporal reasoning or multi-step planning—becomes increasingly costly. An automated, targeted approach to data generation is needed to make model improvement more resource-efficient and precise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically analyzing inference-time failures (e.g., via cost metrics, confidence scores, or consistency checks) and using a base model to synthesize corrective examples, we can generate a small, high-impact training dataset that efficiently improves model performance on the identified weakness categories.
proposals.method: We will build a pipeline that first profiles a target LLM during multi-turn inference, leveraging concepts from Continuum (e.g., tracking KV cache states and failure patterns) to identify recurring error types. For each weakness category, we will use a cost-efficient base model (inspired by BARE) to generate synthetic corrective data, including question-answer pairs and reasoning chains. This data will be filtered and used for fine-tuning, with the process iteratively guided by the profiling system to close performance gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a closed-loop system for automated model refinement, reducing the need for massive, manually curated datasets. It will provide a framework for using inference-time compute and caching strategies not just for serving, but for continuous model diagnosis and improvement, aligning with themes of efficient resource utilization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the typical inference-time scaling paradigm (e.g., Sleep-time Compute) to actively diagnose weaknesses and directly drive targeted data synthesis, creating a tight feedback loop between model deployment profiling and efficient training data generation.

proposals.title: DualBench: Self-Improving Data Curation via Benchmarking the LLM Judge
proposals.motivation: The quality of synthetic data for LLM training hinges on the 'judge' LLM used for curation, yet these judges are typically static and evaluated ad-hoc. As scaling laws push the need for massive, high-quality datasets, a poorly calibrated judge becomes a critical bottleneck and a source of hidden bias. This creates a circular problem: we need good data to train good judges, but we need good judges to curate good data.
proposals.hypothesis: By using a generated benchmark to systematically evaluate the judge LLM's performance and biases, we can create a feedback loop that iteratively improves the judge, leading to higher-quality synthetic data curation and, consequently, more efficient downstream model training.
proposals.method: We will extend methods from BARE to generate a diverse benchmark suite that probes judge failure modes (e.g., preference biases, reasoning errors). This benchmark will be used to compute a multi-faceted 'curator cost' function, quantifying judge accuracy, bias, and compute inefficiency. Inspired by Continuum's resource-aware scheduling, we will design an iterative tuning loop where the judge's outputs on the benchmark guide targeted fine-tuning or prompt optimization, with a cache-and-reuse mechanism for benchmark evaluations to manage compute costs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for evaluating and improving data curation agents, moving beyond static judges. It will yield a practical methodology for creating self-improving curation pipelines, directly addressing data quality as a key constraint in the LLM scaling paradigm. We will release the benchmark generation code and evaluation protocols.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs to judge data, we propose making the judge itself the primary object of optimization using its own generated benchmark. This closes the loop in the curation pipeline, applying resource-aware systems principles from inference scheduling to the data generation problem.

proposals.title: SERVE-Stream: A Unified Architecture for Real-Time Data Ingestion and Low-Latency LLM Prediction Serving
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems are optimized for static inference workloads, creating a costly and latency-inducing disconnect from dynamic, real-time data sources. This separation forces applications to maintain complex, multi-stage pipelines for data ingestion, stream processing, and model serving, increasing operational cost and end-to-end latency for time-sensitive tasks like live analytics and interactive agents. A unified architecture is needed to bridge this gap efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating the prediction serving layer directly with the data ingestion and stream processing logic into a single, resource-aware scheduling system, we can significantly reduce end-to-end latency and computational overhead for real-time LLM applications while maintaining robust serving performance.
proposals.method: We propose to extend the Continuum scheduler's principles to govern both data ingestion streams and LLM inference jobs. The system will feature a unified resource manager that treats incoming data streams and model execution as co-schedulable tasks with shared KV cache management (inspired by Continuum's TTL). We will develop a cost-aware scheduling algorithm that dynamically allocates compute and memory between ingestion processing and model inference, potentially leveraging 'sleep-time compute' for background stream pre-processing. The architecture will be evaluated using synthetic data generation workloads (inspired by BARE) to simulate real-time scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel systems architecture that collapses the traditional separation between data pipelines and model serving, reducing latency and cost for real-time LLM applications. It will deliver a practical, open-source scheduling system and new metrics for evaluating the joint performance of streaming data processing and LLM inference under resource constraints.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes inference (e.g., KV cache management) or data generation separately, this proposal uniquely co-designs the stream processing and LLM serving schedulers into a single, cost-optimized control plane, a departure from the layered, decoupled systems common pre-2026.

proposals.title: StateFlow: A Resource-Aware Orchestration Framework for Stateful LLM Composition with Feedback Loops
proposals.motivation: Current LLM composition frameworks (e.g., LangChain) treat models as largely stateless functions, limiting the development of complex, multi-turn agents that require persistent memory and adaptive control flow. As agents move beyond simple retrieval to tasks involving planning, reflection, and iterative refinement, a principled abstraction for managing state, dependencies, and computational resources is critically needed. This gap hinders the efficient and robust deployment of sophisticated AI applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a declarative, resource-aware orchestration layer for stateful model composition can significantly improve the efficiency, robustness, and expressiveness of multi-step LLM agents by explicitly managing compute, memory (especially KV cache), and complex control dependencies.
proposals.method: We will design StateFlow, a framework extending the Ray/Continuum scheduling paradigm. It introduces a declarative graph DSL where nodes are stateful model instances (encapsulating their KV cache and session state) and edges define dataflow and feedback dependencies. A central scheduler, informed by cost models from 'Sleep-time Compute' and TTL policies from 'Continuum', will manage the lifecycle of stateful nodes, dynamically persisting/evicting KV caches and compute states to optimize for aggregate metrics like cost-per-session or latency. We will implement feedback loops as special cyclic edges with conditional guards, managed by the scheduler to prevent deadlock.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal model and open-source system for stateful, cyclic LLM composition; 2) novel scheduling policies that jointly optimize for inference cost, memory footprint, and responsiveness in long-running agent sessions; 3) empirical validation on complex benchmarks like iterative code generation and debate-based reasoning, demonstrating improved cost-effectiveness and robustness over stateless orchestration.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes single-model inference or stateless pipelines, StateFlow is the first to integrate KV cache and compute state management directly into a declarative composition model, enabling efficient scheduling of agents with complex, cyclic workflows.

proposals.title: REACTOR: Resource-Efficient Adaptive Caching for Online Reinforcement Learning Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large-scale online reinforcement learning (RL) models for interactive agents (e.g., in robotics, gaming, or recommendation systems) presents unique serving challenges distinct from standard LLM inference. These models require low-latency processing of state-action-reward trajectories and must maintain mutable, evolving state across potentially long-lived sessions, conflicting with the stateless, prompt-response paradigm of current KV cache management systems. Efficiently supporting this workload is critical for the next generation of adaptive AI systems but is underexplored in serving system design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending KV cache semantics with a time-to-live (TTL) mechanism governed by RL-specific metrics (e.g., value function uncertainty or policy entropy) and integrating lightweight feedback loops for state updates, we can significantly improve the compute and memory efficiency of serving online RL models without sacrificing latency or decision quality.
proposals.method: We will design REACTOR, a scheduler and cache manager integrated into a system like Continuum. The core innovation is a two-level cache: a standard KV cache for the transformer backbone and a 'State-Action Cache' for trajectory segments. A TTL for each cache entry will be dynamically set by a small, learned meta-model that predicts the future relevance of a state based on RL signals like reward variance and temporal difference error. We will implement a feedback API to allow the RL environment to signal state transitions, triggering cache updates or invalidations. The scheduler will prioritize pre-filling caches for active RL sessions based on predicted action cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel serving system architecture specifically optimized for the iterative, stateful nature of online RL inference, 2) a formalization of RL-aware caching policies that tie resource management to learning-theoretic quantities, and 3) an open-source implementation and evaluation demonstrating reduced p95 latency and improved throughput for RL workloads compared to adapted LLM serving systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Continuum introduces TTL for multi-turn dialogue, applying and dynamically controlling such mechanisms for the non-stationary, feedback-driven state evolution of online RL is novel. Furthermore, co-designing the cache lifecycle with the RL algorithm's internal signals, rather than just request patterns, is a significant departure from existing inference optimization.

proposals.title: The Pareto-Optimal Scheduler: A Formal Framework for Latency-Accuracy Trade-offs in Adaptive LLM Cascades
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) for real-time services faces a fundamental tension between response quality and latency. While adaptive policies like cascades or speculative execution dynamically select models (e.g., small vs. large) to balance this trade-off, current systems are designed heuristically. A formal, generalizable analysis of the optimal latency-accuracy frontier is missing, limiting our ability to make principled resource allocation decisions for cost-effective inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latency-accuracy trade-off for adaptive LLM selection can be formally modeled as a constrained optimization problem, and that its Pareto-optimal frontier can be characterized and approximated efficiently using online learning algorithms that account for request complexity and system state.
proposals.method: We will model the system as a scheduler choosing from a set of LLMs (varying in size/speed) per request. We will formalize accuracy as a task-dependent reward function and latency as a stochastic cost dependent on model choice and dynamic system load (inspired by Continuum's TTL-aware scheduling). Using techniques from online convex optimization and bandit theory, we will develop an algorithm, ParetoCache, that learns to navigate the trade-off frontier. It will integrate a KV cache resource model and a lightweight complexity estimator (potentially using a base model as in BARE) to predict the need for a larger model, adapting its policy based on a user-specified latency budget or accuracy target.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general theoretical framework for analyzing latency-accuracy trade-offs in adaptive LLM serving. It will yield a principled, tunable scheduling algorithm that outperforms heuristic baselines. The resulting analysis will offer system designers clear guidance on configuring model cascades under varying load and performance requirements.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Continuum) optimizes cache utilization and Sleep-time Compute leverages idle cycles, our proposal uniquely formalizes the core latency-accuracy Pareto frontier itself as an optimizable object. This moves beyond heuristic policy design to a foundational theory of adaptive inference, integrating learning, resource constraints, and statistical performance.

proposals.title: Inverse: A System for Efficient, Cost-Aware Collection of Training Data from LLM Prediction Logs
proposals.motivation: The scaling of large language models is critically dependent on high-quality training data, yet current data collection pipelines are often ad-hoc and inefficient. While significant research, including Stoica's own work on inference-time compute and KV cache management, focuses on optimizing the prediction (forward) path, the complementary problem of systematically harvesting valuable training signals from live model deployments remains under-explored. An automated system for this 'inverse' path could dramatically reduce the cost and latency of the data flywheel, enabling more adaptive and performant models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by treating training data collection as a resource-constrained systems problem—applying principles of cost-aware filtering, caching, and scheduling to prediction logs—we can build a system that selectively gathers data of higher learning utility per unit of collection and storage cost than naive logging.
proposals.method: We will design Inverse as a lightweight middleware service that sits between LLM inference endpoints and data warehouses. It will employ: 1) A utility estimator, inspired by few-shot synthetic data generation techniques like BARE, to score the potential value of a logged query-response pair for future model training. 2) A cost-aware scheduler, leveraging concepts from Continuum's TTL mechanisms, to manage write-amplification and storage costs by dynamically adjusting sampling rates and retention policies based on data 'freshness' and utility. 3) A compact, indexed cache for frequent or high-utility patterns to avoid redundant storage, extending ideas from KV cache optimization to the data management layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, open-source system for closing the loop between inference and training. It will yield concrete metrics on the cost-utility trade-offs in training data collection and demonstrate how systematic logging can accelerate model improvement cycles in production settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing training data collection not as a simple logging task but as a structured resource allocation problem, applying systems techniques from inference optimization (caching, scheduling, cost modeling) to the fundamentally different challenge of efficient data harvesting.

proposals.title: Latency-Aware KV Cache Management for Real-Time LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: Current methods for scaling LLM inference, such as continuous batching and speculative execution, are optimized for high-throughput, batch-oriented settings like offline data processing. However, emerging latency-sensitive applications—including real-time conversational agents, interactive coding assistants, and live translation—require sub-second response times under unpredictable, single-stream request patterns. Adapting high-throughput techniques directly to these scenarios often fails, as they introduce queuing delays and prioritize aggregate compute efficiency over individual request latency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a latency-aware KV cache management policy, which dynamically prioritizes and evicts cache states based on real-time request criticality and predicted future load, can significantly reduce tail latency for single-stream LLM inference without substantially increasing total compute cost.
proposals.method: We will design a scheduler that extends the Time-to-Live (TTL) concept from Continuum with a latency-critical, priority-aware eviction policy. The system will monitor per-request latency SLOs and real-time system load to dynamically adjust the KV cache 'holding' priority for multi-turn sessions. We will integrate this with a cost model for Sleep-time Compute, strategically scheduling non-latency-critical background pre-computation or fine-tuning tasks during idle periods to warm the cache for anticipated high-priority requests, thereby reducing their time-to-first-token.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and system implementation for low-latency LLM serving, bridging the gap between batch-optimized scaling research and real-time application needs. We will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating latency-sensitive LLM inference and demonstrate how to co-schedule latency-critical and background tasks efficiently within a unified resource pool.
proposals.novelty: While prior work manages KV cache for throughput, our approach explicitly models and optimizes for tail latency and request priority in a dynamic, single-stream setting. Furthermore, it novelly co-designs proactive Sleep-time Compute with reactive cache management to prepare for latency spikes.

proposals.title: FlowStream: A Pipelined Dataflow Execution Model for Overlapping LLM Inference, Memory, and Compute
proposals.motivation: Current LLM inference is bottlenecked by sequential token generation and static KV cache management, leading to high latency and underutilized compute resources. As models scale and multi-turn agent applications proliferate, these inefficiencies in time, compute, and memory become critical barriers to cost-effective deployment. A new execution model is needed to better orchestrate these interdependent resources.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling LLM inference as a streaming dataflow graph where token generation, KV cache operations, and external tool calls are independent, parallelizable stages, we can significantly reduce end-to-end latency and improve system throughput through strategic overlapping.
proposals.method: We will design a dataflow execution engine where each transformer layer is a node in a pipeline. We will extend the Continuum 'Time-to-Live' concept to manage cache eviction as a background dataflow stage. Crucially, we will integrate 'sleep-time' compute opportunities, like speculative execution for future turns based on BARE-style synthetic data generation, into the pipeline's slack periods. Scheduling will use a cost model to dynamically reorder and parallelize dataflow operators based on real-time resource constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel LLM serving system that demonstrably lowers latency and cost per token by maximizing hardware utilization. It will provide a formal framework for reasoning about overlapping operations in LLM inference, bridging concepts from parallel computing and modern LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: While pipelining exists for model parallelism, applying fine-grained, asynchronous dataflow principles to overlap token generation, dynamic cache management, and background 'sleep-time' compute within a single inference stream is unexplored. This moves beyond static scheduling to a reactive, resource-aware execution model.

proposals.title: TRAIN-CACHE: Aggregating and Offloading Memory-Compute States for Efficient Large-Scale LLM Training
proposals.motivation: The training of large language models is fundamentally constrained by GPU memory, forcing a trade-off between model size, batch size, and sequence length. Current methods like gradient checkpointing and pipeline parallelism introduce significant computational overhead or communication complexity. A new approach is needed to directly optimize the memory-compute trade-off during the forward and backward passes, analogous to innovations like KV caching for inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying principles of temporal aggregation, caching, and strategic offloading—inspired by inference-time optimizations—to intermediate activations and gradient states during training, we can significantly reduce peak memory usage without proportionally increasing wall-clock training time.
proposals.method: We will design a scheduler that profiles the computational graph of a transformer training step to identify states (e.g., intermediate activations, layer outputs) with high recomputation cost but low memory footprint. Inspired by Continuum's Time-to-Live concept, we will implement a managed cache for these states, dynamically deciding to keep them in GPU memory, offload them to CPU/NVMe, or recompute them based on a cost model integrating access frequency, recomputation FLOPs, and I/O latency. This will extend the 'Sleep-time Compute' paradigm to leverage idle periods in distributed training (e.g., during communication or synchronization) for proactive prefetching of offloaded states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for memory-compute optimization in LLM training, enabling larger effective batch sizes or longer context windows on fixed hardware. We will release an open-source library integrating with PyTorch, and empirically demonstrate reduced training time or increased model scale on benchmarks like the Pile.
proposals.novelty: While caching and offloading are well-studied for inference, applying a dynamic, cost-model-driven caching system to the distinct temporal and dependency patterns of the training backward pass is a novel direction. It moves beyond static recomputation to an adaptive, resource-aware management of the training state.

proposals.title: Beyond Memory Walls: Inference-Time Model Scaling via Hierarchical Activation Caching and Compute-Resource Scheduling
proposals.motivation: As LLMs scale beyond 175B parameters, single-GPU inference becomes impossible due to memory constraints, primarily from the KV cache. Existing methods like quantization and offloading trade off significant latency for memory, hindering real-time applications. This work addresses the critical challenge of enabling the use of frontier-scale models on constrained, single-device hardware without prohibitive latency penalties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by treating model parameters as a persistent, high-latency memory and activations as a compute resource, we can schedule inference across time and a hierarchical cache to run models significantly larger than GPU memory limits at acceptable throughput.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Sleep-time Compute' and 'Continuum' frameworks to create a hierarchical caching system. A Time-to-Live (TTL) policy will manage a fast GPU cache for hot KV states and a slow CPU/disk cache for cold states. A resource-aware scheduler, guided by a learned cost model, will prefetch and evict model layers and KV cache blocks, overlapping compute with memory transfers. We will implement and evaluate this system by loading and running inference with models exceeding 500B parameters on a single A100 GPU.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical system for single-GPU inference of ultra-large models, expanding access to frontier AI. It will contribute a formal model for the trade-off between compute scheduling, cache hierarchy, and inference latency, advancing the field of resource-efficient LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of KV cache management with a scheduler that treats entire model layers as schedulable, cacheable resources over time, moving beyond static offloading or simple cache eviction towards a holistic, latency-aware resource scheduling system for inference.

proposals.title: PagedKV: Virtual Memory Management for On-Device LLM Inference Under Extreme Memory Constraints
proposals.motivation: Deploying LLMs on mobile and edge devices is critical for privacy, latency, and accessibility, but is severely limited by device DRAM capacity, which is insufficient for full KV caches of even small models. Current inference optimizations, like vLLM's PagedAttention, assume server-grade memory and batch processing, making them unsuitable for single-stream, memory-starved edge environments where traditional caching fails.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a virtual memory system for the KV cache, explicitly designed for single-query inference with no batching, can enable LLM operation on devices with memory smaller than the model's nominal cache requirement by efficiently swapping cache pages between limited DRAM and slower, but larger, storage (e.g., flash).
proposals.method: We will design PagedKV, a lightweight inference engine extending PagedAttention principles. It will implement a fine-grained, block-level KV cache that treats device DRAM as a physical cache and flash storage as virtual memory. A Time-to-Live (TTL) and attention-score-aware eviction policy (inspired by Continuum) will prioritize retaining critical attention heads. We will integrate selective recomputation strategies (akin to Sleep-time Compute) for evicted high-impact pages to manage the flash latency penalty, optimizing the total cost of memory and compute.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical system for running LLMs on resource-constrained devices, expanding their deployment frontier. It will provide a formal framework for analyzing the memory-compute-storage trade-off in single-stream inference and release an open-source prototype benchmarked against real mobile hardware.
proposals.novelty: While PagedAttention manages memory for *batched* inference, PagedKV is the first to redesign virtual memory management explicitly for the singular pressure point of *unbatched, on-device* inference, co-designing eviction policies with flash-aware recomputation schedules.

proposals.title: Inverse Scaling: Optimal LLM Scheduling Under a Hard Memory Constraint
proposals.motivation: As LLMs grow, the primary bottleneck for deployment is often memory, not compute, especially for multi-tenant inference services or on-device applications. Existing scaling laws and scheduling work (e.g., Continuum's KV cache TTL) optimize for latency or throughput given compute, but lack a principled framework for the inverse problem: maximizing useful compute and model capability when memory is the fixed, scarce resource. This directly addresses cost and resource challenges in real-world, constrained systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating LLM inference as a scheduling problem with a memory budget, we can design algorithms that dynamically manage model loading, KV cache retention, and request batching to maximize computational throughput and effectively support larger models than a naive static allocation would allow.
proposals.method: We will develop a scheduler that treats the memory budget as a constraint in a mixed-integer optimization problem, deciding which model parameters to keep resident, which KV caches to evict (extending Continuum's TTL concept with cost-aware policies), and how to batch queries. The core will be a lightweight profiler that estimates the compute utility (e.g., tokens/sec per GB) of different model partitions and cache states, enabling greedy or reinforcement learning-based scheduling. We will integrate this with a system like vLLM or HuggingFace TGI for evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for 'inverse' scaling, yielding practical scheduling algorithms for memory-constrained LLM serving. It will expand the Pareto frontier of model size vs. throughput under fixed memory, directly lowering deployment costs. We will also release a benchmark for evaluating memory-constrained scheduling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes compute given performance goals, this inverts the problem to maximize compute utility under a hard memory cap, a fundamental shift in perspective. It uniquely combines model swapping, dynamic KV cache management, and batching into a single constrained optimization, moving beyond isolated cache policies.

proposals.title: PrecisionBench: A Cost-Aware Framework for High-Quality, Expert-Curated Data Subsetting and Evaluation
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in LLM development (pre-2026) prioritizes scaling training data size, often at the expense of quality and annotation precision, leading to inefficient resource use and noisy benchmarks. This creates a critical need to rigorously assess whether a small, expertly curated dataset can provide superior evaluation signal and training efficiency compared to massive, unrefined corpora, directly challenging the 'scale-is-all' assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, expertly annotated dataset, curated with strategic sampling from a large corpus, can yield more precise, reliable, and cost-effective model benchmarking and targeted fine-tuning than orders-of-magnitude larger, noisy datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a cost-aware curation algorithm that selects a minimal, high-value subset from a large dataset (e.g., a slice of The Pile or C4). The algorithm will optimize for information density and annotation confidence, leveraging base LLMs (inspired by BARE) for initial candidate filtering and uncertainty estimation. Expert annotators will then provide high-fidelity labels for this subset. We will benchmark models on this 'PrecisionBench' versus the full noisy set, analyzing performance correlation, variance, and the compute/memory cost (extending themes from Sleep-time Compute and Continuum) per unit of evaluation insight.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a rigorous, quantitative framework for valuing dataset quality over scale, 2) a publicly released, expertly annotated benchmark suite for precise capability measurement, and 3) practical guidelines for cost-efficient data curation that reduces inference and training resource overhead.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges a core scaling assumption by framing dataset value through a cost-quality-inference lens, combining expert curation with algorithmic subset selection optimized for evaluation efficiency rather than just training loss.

proposals.title: Sparrow: A Cost-Aware, Stateful Router for Efficient Mixture of Specialized Language Models
proposals.motivation: The dominant scaling paradigm for large language models (LLMs) relies on ever-larger generalist models, leading to prohibitive inference costs and memory footprints for widespread deployment. A promising alternative is a system of many smaller, task-specific models, but efficiently routing queries among them—balancing accuracy, latency, and resource utilization—remains an open and critical systems challenge. This directly addresses the core themes of compute, memory, and inference cost in Stoica's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learned router, co-designed with a time-to-live (TTL) mechanism for managing agent state and KV cache, can dynamically dispatch queries to a collection of specialized models, achieving higher quality-cost trade-offs than a single monolithic LLM for a diverse workload of user requests.
proposals.method: We will design Sparrow, a router trained via reinforcement learning to optimize a combined objective of task accuracy and a cost function incorporating inference latency, compute cycles, and memory (including KV cache footprint). Sparrow will interact with a pool of specialized models, each with a Continuum-like scheduler managing its KV cache TTL. The router's state will include query embeddings, model performance profiles, and real-time system load, enabling decisions that consider both semantic fit and resource availability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel routing architecture that makes explicit cost-resource trade-offs for model specialization, 2) an empirical demonstration that a routed system of smaller models can outperform a single large model on aggregate metrics of quality and efficiency, and 3) a systems framework integrating scheduling (Continuum) and cost-aware decision-making for sustainable LLM deployment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on mixture-of-experts or static task routing, Sparrow integrates real-time resource and cache state into the routing decision, treating the pool of models as a schedulable compute continuum rather than a static ensemble. This moves beyond pure inference scaling to holistic resource management at test-time.

proposals.title: Orchestra: Control-Flow-Aware Learned KV Cache Management for Efficient LLM Agent Scheduling
proposals.motivation: The orchestration of LLMs into complex, stateful workflows with loops and conditionals is critical for advanced applications, but current KV cache management is sequence-based, oblivious to high-level program structure. This leads to inefficient recomputation and memory blow-up for recurrent logic, severely limiting the scale and cost-effectiveness of programmable LLM agents. Pre-2026 systems like Continuum introduce time-based eviction but lack semantic awareness of the orchestration control flow that dictates cache utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a KV cache manager trained with explicit signals from a high-level orchestration language's control-flow graph can learn to predict, allocate, and reuse cache states with far greater efficiency than sequence-based or heuristic methods, enabling near-constant memory for loops and optimal reuse across branches.
proposals.method: We will extend a frontend language (e.g., based on SGLang or DSPy) to emit a real-time control-flow graph (CFG) and program state trace. This trace, alongside token sequences, will train a lightweight neural cache manager (a GNN or small transformer) to predict the future utility of any KV cache entry. The manager will integrate with a modified continuous batching scheduler, implementing learned, dynamic cache allocation and a 'Time-to-Live' informed by the CFG. We will develop algorithms for cache checkpointing at loop headers and semantic similarity-based merging for conditional branches.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, control-flow-aware learned cache manager that reduces memory and recompute costs in LLM orchestration, 2) an open-source integration of this manager with an existing orchestration framework, and 3) empirical evidence showing order-of-magnitude improvements in memory efficiency for workflows with loops and branches over state-of-the-art methods like vLLM and Continuum.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is using the formal semantics of a high-level orchestration language—specifically its control-flow constructs—as a direct training signal and runtime guide for a learned cache manager, moving beyond token-based prediction to program-structure-based prediction.

proposals.title: Coordinator-Free LM Agents: A Gossip Protocol for Real-Time, Decentralized LLM Execution
proposals.motivation: Current multi-agent and complex LM program systems rely on a central orchestrator for task dispatch and state management, creating a single point of latency and failure. This centralized bottleneck fundamentally limits the real-time interactivity required for immersive human-AI collaboration and responsive multi-agent coordination. A decentralized execution model is needed to eliminate scheduling overhead and enable direct, low-latency communication between agent peers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a coordination-free, gossip-based protocol can enable efficient and robust decentralized execution of LM tasks, achieving lower and more predictable latency for interactive sessions than centralized schedulers, without sacrificing correctness in agent communication.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight gossip protocol where agent nodes broadcast their capabilities and resource states (compute, memory, KV cache status). Tasks (e.g., tool calls, sub-queries) are dynamically partitioned and dispatched via direct peer-to-peer requests based on a real-time cost model, inspired by Continuum's TTL concepts for resource management. Communication employs a shared, versioned blackboard with conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) for state synchronization, and we will leverage BARE-style synthetic data to train small router models for intelligent task dispatch.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel system architecture for decentralized LM agent execution, a formal protocol for coordination-free task dispatch, and empirical evidence that this approach reduces tail latency in interactive sessions. It provides a foundational layer for building highly scalable, real-time collaborative AI systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on centralized multi-agent schedulers or server-side KV cache optimizations, this proposal eliminates the central coordinator entirely, applying gossip and CRDT principles from distributed systems directly to the runtime of LM programs for real-time interaction.

proposals.title: LIVE: Low-Latency Interactive Virtualization Engine for Single-Stream LLM Execution
proposals.motivation: Current LLM serving systems, like vLLM and TGI, are optimized for high-throughput batch processing, prioritizing aggregate compute efficiency over individual user latency. This creates a poor fit for interactive, single-user applications such as real-time coding assistants, conversational agents, and creative tools, where sub-100ms token latency is critical for user experience. The batch-first paradigm inherently introduces queueing delays and resource contention, wasting cycles on speculative batching for inherently sporadic interactive requests.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing an execution runtime that treats a single, interactive LLM program as a first-class, continuously executing process with guaranteed resource reservations, we can achieve order-of-magnitude reductions in token latency compared to batched schedulers, without proportional increases in compute cost per token.
proposals.method: We will design LIVE, a runtime that virtualizes a dedicated, persistent compute context (CPU/GPU) for a single LLM program, pre-allocating its maximum potential KV cache (inspired by Continuum's TTL management) and memory. It employs a just-in-time, streaming-aware scheduler that bypasses global batching queues, integrating techniques from Sleep-time Compute to utilize idle cycles for background pre-filling or state maintenance. We will develop a lightweight cost model to dynamically adjust this reservation's footprint based on the program's recent activity and predicted demand.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new systems blueprint for latency-critical LLM applications, demonstrating that the cost of guaranteed low latency can be managed. We will release an open-source prototype and a benchmark showing latency distributions for interactive tasks versus state-of-the-art batched systems, challenging the assumption that batch processing is always the most efficient paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes batch scheduling or KV cache management within the batch paradigm, LIVE fundamentally rejects batch-first execution for a designated class of workloads, proposing a virtualization approach that treats an LLM agent like a long-running, low-latency daemon process, a concept unexplored in pre-2026 serving systems.

proposals.title: ArchGraph: Automated Kernel Synthesis for Efficient Inference on Emerging LLM Architectures
proposals.motivation: The rapid emergence of novel LLM architectures, such as State Space Models (e.g., Mamba) and Mixture-of-Experts models, creates a performance portability crisis. Systems like SGLang offer significant inference speedups for transformer-based models by fusing operations and optimizing KV cache management, but these gains are lost on non-transformer models, requiring costly manual kernel re-engineering. This limits the adoption of efficient architectures and increases inference cost and latency for new model families.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the computational graph of any autoregressive language model can be decomposed into a small set of fundamental, architecture-agnostic patterns (e.g., recurrent scans, dynamic routing, attention), and that these patterns can be automatically mapped to a library of pre-optimized, composable primitives to generate a near-optimal custom runtime.
proposals.method: We will develop a static analysis pass that profiles a PyTorch/JAX model to extract its core computational patterns and dataflow. A pattern-matching engine, informed by a taxonomy of operations (scan, route, gather-scatter, etc.), will match subgraphs to kernels in an expanded SGLang library, which we will extend with primitives for SSMs and conditional computation. A synthesis module will then generate a fused execution plan, incorporating optimizations like state caching (inspired by Continuum's TTL) and just-in-time kernel compilation, validated for correctness via differential testing against the original model.
proposals.contribution: 1) A system that automatically brings SGLang-level efficiency to diverse, non-transformer LLM architectures, reducing inference latency and cost. 2) A formalized taxonomy of LLM computational patterns and a method for safe, correct graph-to-kernel mapping. 3) An expanded open-source kernel library for next-generation models, decoupling architectural innovation from systems optimization.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond manual porting or one-off compiler optimizations by introducing a pattern-based, architecture-agnostic intermediate representation for LLM inference, enabling automatic specialization. It combines graph-level analysis with a library of high-efficiency, composable primitives—a synthesis not explored in prior model-serving or compilation systems.

proposals.title: ReFlow: An Incremental Constraint Solver for Reactive LLM Orchestration
proposals.motivation: Current LLM orchestration frameworks (e.g., LangChain) treat workflows as static, predefined graphs, which is ill-suited for adaptive agentic systems. As agents perform long-running tasks, they must react to new information, dynamically decompose goals, and adjust resource allocation, but existing systems cause expensive recomputation and poor cache utilization. This inefficiency limits the complexity and cost-effectiveness of autonomous, stateful agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling LLM orchestration control flow as a reactive, incremental constraint satisfaction problem will enable efficient execution of adaptive workflows, significantly reducing redundant computation and latency while preserving developer intent.
proposals.method: We will design a domain-specific language (DSL) for specifying stateful control flow patterns (loops, branches, recursion) as constraints over a dynamically evolving computation graph. A runtime scheduler, building on ideas from Continuum's KV Cache TTL and Sleep-time Compute, will integrate an incremental SAT/constraint solver (e.g., MiniSAT with incremental API). This solver will react to graph changes—triggered by LLM outputs or external events—by efficiently updating the execution schedule, reusing cached intermediate states (KV caches, tool results) where constraints permit, and minimizing new LLM invocations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new programming model and runtime for adaptive LLM agents, demonstrating substantial reductions in token cost and latency for complex workflows. It will provide a formal framework for reasoning about computational reuse in reactive systems, advancing the state of efficient LLM inference and orchestration.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static DAG compilers or fixed-policy schedulers, ReFlow introduces a *reactive, incremental constraint-solving* backend, treating workflow evolution as a continuous optimization problem. This directly unifies high-level adaptive control flow with low-level resource management (cache, compute) in a way pre-2026 orchestration systems do not.

proposals.title: Metabolic Control for Machine Learning: Dynamic Resource Orchestration in Multi-Modal Pipelines
proposals.motivation: Multi-modal AI pipelines, integrating vision, language, and other models, are becoming standard for complex tasks. However, their static resource allocation leads to inefficiency, as computational bottlenecks shift dynamically based on input and intermediate state. Pre-2026, optimizing such pipelines for cost and latency remains a manual or heuristic-driven challenge, lacking a principled framework for real-time adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a multi-modal pipeline as a computational metabolic network and applying principles from Metabolic Control Analysis, we can derive a control-theoretic model that dynamically reallocates finite compute and memory resources to maximize end-to-end pipeline efficiency under varying conditions.
proposals.method: We will formalize pipeline components as 'enzymes' and data flow as 'flux'. Inspired by cybernetic modeling, we will develop online estimators for control coefficients, quantifying each component's impact on overall latency/throughput. A lightweight scheduler, informed by these coefficients and real-time monitoring of KV cache states and compute loads (extending concepts from Continuum and Sleep-time Compute), will dynamically adjust resource budgets (e.g., GPU allocation, batch size, cache TTL). We will validate the framework on pipelines built from open-source vision-language models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, bio-inspired framework for adaptive resource management in composite AI systems. It will provide both a theoretical analogy linking systems biology to distributed systems and a practical scheduler that reduces inference cost and latency for multi-modal pipelines without sacrificing robustness.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct translation of Metabolic Control Analysis to computational resource allocation, moving beyond traditional queueing theory or reinforcement learning. It introduces the concept of 'computational control coefficients' for real-time bottleneck identification in AI pipelines.

proposals.title: ALOHA: Adaptive Learning of Heterogeneous Action Costs for LLM Program Optimization
proposals.motivation: LLM-based agents and programs increasingly chain complex reasoning steps and tool calls, but current planners use static, inaccurate cost heuristics for latency, token use, and success probability. This leads to inefficient execution as real costs are non-stationary, varying with model version, prompt context, and external API load. An optimizer that learns and adapts to these dynamic costs in real-time is crucial for scalable, cost-effective agent deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling LLM program execution as an online learning problem with bandit-style exploration will enable an optimizer to discover and exploit high-efficiency plans, significantly reducing average execution cost and improving robustness to runtime variations compared to static planners.
proposals.method: We will develop a system where a meta-optimizer treats each reasoning primitive or tool as an 'arm' with initially uncertain cost distributions. It will employ a Thompson Sampling strategy to explore alternative program structures, instrumenting each step to collect latency, token counts, and correctness feedback. These observations will update Gaussian Process or Bayesian neural network cost models, which will then guide future planning via a Monte Carlo Tree Search that balances estimated cost and uncertainty. The system will integrate with a KV cache manager (like Continuum) to account for memory resource costs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel online learning framework for adaptive LLM program optimization and an open-source implementation demonstrating reduced average cost per task. It will provide the first empirical study of non-stationary cost dynamics in multi-step LM inference and tool use, offering new benchmarks for agent efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Sleep-time Compute) optimizes fixed compute graphs, our approach uniquely learns cost models online from execution traces, adapting to prompt-specific and temporal variations—a shift from static optimization to adaptive, feedback-driven control.

proposals.title: Cerebro: A Self-Optimizing LLM Runtime via Trace Decompilation and Plan Caching
proposals.motivation: The cost of serving large language models (LLMs) is dominated by inference-time compute and memory, particularly for complex, multi-turn agentic workloads. Current systems like SGLang or vLLM optimize individual execution strategies but lack a principled, cost-based framework to synthesize and cache optimal execution plans for recurring computational patterns, leading to repeated suboptimal resource use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system, which decompiles low-level inference traces into high-level, composable programs and applies a cost-based optimizer to plan and cache their execution, can automatically discover and reuse highly efficient execution strategies, significantly reducing average inference cost for repetitive workloads.
proposals.method: We will build Cerebro, a runtime system with two core components. First, a neural decompiler (inspired by BARE) will map execution traces (operator sequences, KV cache states) back to structured programs in an SGLang-like IR. Second, a cost-based optimizer (extending ideas from Sleep-time Compute and Continuum) will enumerate alternative plans for these programs, evaluating trade-offs in parallelization, KV cache Time-to-Live, and speculative decoding using a learned cost model. The most efficient plan for a given program fingerprint will be stored in a global plan cache, creating a feedback loop where successful executions refine the cost model and program representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel methodology for cost-optimized LLM program synthesis, 2) the Cerebro system demonstrating automatic plan discovery and reuse, and 3) empirical evidence that closed-loop optimization can drive down inference costs for agentic workloads beyond static compilation or single-query optimization.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of neural trace decompilation with database-style cost-based optimization and plan caching creates a self-improving loop for LLM execution, a direction unexplored in pre-2026 inference serving systems which treat optimization and execution as separate, static stages.

proposals.title: FuseTile: Applying Loop Fusion and Tiling from HPC to Optimize Kernel Fusion for LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: The inference cost of large language models is dominated by memory bandwidth, particularly the I/O of intermediate tensors between small, discrete GPU kernels. Existing kernel fusion in compilers like TVM or Triton reduces launches but often creates sub-optimal fused kernels that do not maximize data locality in on-chip memory. This inefficiency directly impacts the cost, latency, and resource utilization of serving LLMs at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly decoupling the fusion of operation subgraphs from the subsequent tiling of the fused computation, a methodology adapted from high-performance scientific computing can produce more efficient macro-kernels that significantly increase computational intensity and reduce global memory traffic for core LLM operations like attention blocks.
proposals.method: First, we will use a graph-level compiler pass to identify and fuse coherent subgraphs (e.g., the combined QKV projection, attention scoring, and output projection) into single, large macro-kernels. Second, we will develop a tile optimizer using a polyhedral model or heuristic search to automatically determine optimal thread block and tile dimensions for these novel fused kernels, explicitly targeting register and shared memory capacity. This will be implemented and evaluated within an existing compiler stack like PyTorch's Inductor or a custom Triton backend.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a systematic compiler optimization for LLM inference that demonstrably lowers latency and increases throughput by minimizing off-chip data movement. It will also contribute a new perspective on applying classical HPC loop transformations to the emerging problem of deep learning kernel engineering.
proposals.novelty: While kernel fusion is known, the explicit two-stage separation—fusion followed by dedicated polyhedral-style tiling for the resulting macro-kernel—is a novel adaptation of the classic HPC optimization pipeline, which has not been systematically applied to the irregular but structured computation graphs of transformer-based LLMs.

proposals.title: Metabolic Compilation: A Constraint-Based Optimization Approach for Synthesizing Minimal SGLang Runtimes on Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: Deploying tiny language models (TLMs) on microcontrollers and edge devices is critical for ubiquitous, private, and low-latency AI, but is severely limited by memory and compute constraints. Existing inference runtimes like SGLang are designed for generality, carrying significant binary size and runtime overhead unsuitable for extreme edge hardware. A systematic, automated method to generate ultra-minimal, hardware- and model-specific runtimes is needed to unlock new applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the SGLang runtime as a computational metabolic network and applying Flux Balance Analysis (FBA)-inspired constraint-based optimization, we can automatically synthesize a functionally complete yet minimal runtime kernel set for a given TLM and hardware target, outperforming manually crafted minimal runtimes in binary size and efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a compiler that takes a hardware profile (e.g., ESP32 memory hierarchy, ISA) and a TLM's computational graph as inputs. It will construct a directed hypergraph representing the runtime, where nodes are operations (kernels, memory ops) and edges are data dependencies. A Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solver will then perform constraint-based optimization, with objectives to minimize binary size and latency, subject to functional correctness constraints ensuring the graph can be executed. The output is a tailored C++ source code for the 'core' runtime, which is then cross-compiled.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel constraint-based framework for automatic inference runtime specialization, 2) a significant reduction in binary footprint and runtime overhead for TLMs on edge devices versus baseline SGLang, and 3) an open-source toolchain enabling broader deployment of efficient edge LLM agents.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the application of systems biology optimization techniques (FBA) to the systems problem of runtime synthesis, moving beyond manual pruning or heuristic compilation to a principled, solver-driven approach for generating minimal viable runtimes.

proposals.title: MicroSGL: A Minimal, Cache-Aware Runtime for Efficient Tiny Language Model Inference on Microcontrollers
proposals.motivation: The deployment of small language models (SLMs) on microcontrollers and edge devices is hindered by the high memory and compute overhead of existing inference runtimes, which are designed for server-grade hardware. Pre-2026, frameworks like SGLang optimize for latency and throughput on GPUs but are ill-suited for devices with kilobytes of RAM and no OS. Enabling efficient on-device SLM inference is critical for pervasive, private, and responsive intelligent applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a runtime from first principles for microcontroller constraints—explicitly managing KV cache lifetime and exploiting sleep-time compute—we can achieve viable inference latency and memory footprint for sub-100M parameter models on resource-constrained edge devices.
proposals.method: We will develop MicroSGL, a stripped-down C++ runtime building on SGLang's core ideas but tailored for microcontrollers. It will implement a static, ahead-of-time graph compiler to eliminate runtime parsing overhead. Inspired by Continuum, we will integrate a deterministic KV Cache Time-to-Live (TTL) policy to bound memory use. We will leverage sleep-time compute concepts for opportunistic batch processing of queued requests during device idle periods, managed by a lightweight scheduler.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, production-ready inference runtime specifically for microcontroller-class devices running modern SLMs. It will provide a systematic evaluation of the trade-offs between model size, KV cache policies, and latency on real hardware (e.g., ESP32, RP2040), establishing a new benchmark for edge LLM deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes KV cache for large models on servers, MicroSGL uniquely co-designs the cache lifetime, execution graph, and sleep-time scheduler for the extreme memory and power constraints of microcontrollers, a domain largely unexplored by existing LLM systems research.

proposals.title: FlowSync: Adaptive Runtime Orchestration for Model-Parallel and Mixture-of-Experts Training
proposals.motivation: Training large-scale model-parallel and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models is hampered by dynamic runtime inefficiencies. Static partitioning of computation and communication leads to suboptimal hardware utilization due to fluctuating load imbalances, network contention, and the variable routing of tokens to experts, directly increasing the cost and time of training. A system that can dynamically adapt the execution plan in response to observed bottlenecks is needed to maximize throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, feedback-driven orchestrator, inspired by principles of adaptive traffic control, can dynamically reconfigure task graphs and communication schedules to mitigate runtime contention, thereby significantly reducing per-step training time for distributed LLMs compared to static schedules.
proposals.method: We will design a runtime system with lightweight profiling agents on each device to monitor compute load, KV cache pressure, and MoE gating decisions. A central coordinator will use this telemetry to model the execution graph as a dynamic network flow problem. It will employ online scheduling algorithms to adjust pipeline execution order, recompute communication paths for all-to-all operations in MoE layers, and potentially shift expert placement, using techniques similar to model-predictive control to proactively avoid bottlenecks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source orchestration layer for distributed LLM training frameworks that improves system throughput. It will provide a formal framework for modeling and dynamically optimizing runtime resource contention in model-parallel and MoE settings, moving beyond static compilation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes static graphs or KV cache management, this proposal introduces a continuous, feedback-driven control loop for the entire distributed training process, explicitly treating compute and communication as a dynamic flow network to be managed in real-time, akin to urban traffic or smart grid systems.

proposals.title: Skyline: A Cost-Aware Runtime for Dynamic Orchestration of Multi-Node Model-Parallel and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs
proposals.motivation: The scaling of LLMs increasingly relies on model parallelism (MP) and mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures to manage memory and compute constraints. However, existing inference runtimes and scheduling systems are not designed to efficiently orchestrate complex, dynamic computational graphs across heterogeneous nodes, leading to suboptimal utilization of expensive GPU clusters and high latency. A unified runtime that can manage both MP's pipeline/tensor parallelism and MoE's conditional execution is critical for cost-effective deployment of large-scale models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cost-aware, dynamic runtime scheduler, which treats MP and MoE subgraphs as first-class primitives and uses predictive models for network and compute latency, can significantly improve aggregate cluster throughput and reduce inference cost compared to static orchestration and monolithic scheduling approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend a distributed systems runtime (e.g., Ray) with a novel scheduler that models the execution of MP and MoE LLM programs as a constrained optimization problem. The scheduler will incorporate a Time-to-Live (TTL) mechanism for KV caches across pipeline stages (inspired by Continuum) and a cost model that factors in inter-node communication, expert routing variance, and GPU memory bandwidth. We will implement a dynamic batching strategy that co-locates compatible subgraphs from different requests, and evaluate using simulated and real clusters with models like GPT-3 (MP) and Switch Transformers (MoE).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) an open-source, production-grade runtime for orchestrating hybrid MP/MoE inference graphs, 2) a formal cost model and scheduling algorithm for multi-tenant LLM serving on shared clusters, and 3) a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating improved throughput-cost trade-offs over existing frameworks like DeepSpeed and TGI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes either MP (e.g., pipeline parallelism) or MoE (e.g., expert load balancing) in isolation, Skyline is novel in its integrated, cost-driven orchestration of both paradigms within a single runtime, treating the entire computational graph as a schedulable resource, akin to how operating systems manage processes.

proposals.title: Ariadne: Co-designing Profiling and Architecture for Massively Concurrent Language Model Simulations
proposals.motivation: Large-scale multi-agent simulations using LLMs are critical for studying emergent social behaviors, testing economic policies, and training agentic systems, but they are severely bottlenecked by compute and memory. Current approaches run thousands of inefficient, monolithic agent programs, leading to prohibitive costs from redundant LLM inference, bloated state, and unoptimized communication. A principled co-design between profiling and system architecture is needed to make massive simulations feasible.
proposals.hypothesis: By profiling unstructured multi-agent code to extract systemic inefficiency patterns (e.g., redundant KV cache generation, repeated context re-computation, wasteful inter-agent messaging), we can co-design a simulation runtime with optimized kernels and primitives that eliminate these bottlenecks, enabling an order-of-magnitude increase in concurrent agents without loss of behavioral fidelity.
proposals.method: We will first build a dynamic profiler that instruments Python-based agent frameworks (e.g., LangChain, AutoGen) to trace computation graphs, KV cache usage, and message traffic, identifying anti-patterns like repeated prompt processing and idle agent memory bloat. Second, inspired by Continuum's TTL-based KV cache management and Sleep-time Compute's background processing, we will design a simulation kernel with a shared, versioned KV cache service, lightweight agent skeletons that offload common reasoning to specialized models, and an event-driven communication bus that batches and compresses messages. The profiler's output will directly parameterize these architectural components.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) an open-source profiler and a taxonomy of inefficiency patterns for LLM-based agents, 2) a high-performance simulation runtime kernel that reduces per-agent compute and memory overhead by exploiting these patterns, and 3) an empirical demonstration scaling to >10k concurrent agents in social simulation scenarios, quantifying the gains from co-design.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that optimizes single-model inference or static KV cache management, Ariadne introduces a closed-loop, profiling-driven co-design where the architecture of the entire simulation system is dynamically adapted based on observed inefficiencies from real, complex agent interactions, creating a self-improving scaling cycle.

proposals.title: ChronoParallel: Co-designing Model Parallelism and KV Cache Management for Extreme-Scale LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: As LLMs scale beyond trillion parameters, model parallelism across thousands of devices becomes essential, but communication overhead for KV states and activations dominates latency and cost. Existing parallelism strategies (e.g., pipeline, tensor) treat communication as a fixed cost, ignoring the temporal and sparsity characteristics of inference workloads, leading to inefficient resource utilization at extreme scales.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing model-parallel partitioning with time-aware, workload-adaptive KV cache management policies, we can significantly reduce communication volume and latency for ultra-large models, enabling efficient inference scaling where traditional approaches fail.
proposals.method: We will develop ChronoParallel, a system that integrates a novel model-parallel scheduler with a KV cache Time-to-Live (TTL) mechanism, inspired by Continuum. The scheduler will use predictive models of layer activation sparsity and inter-device bandwidth to dynamically partition the model graph, minimizing synchronous communication. Concurrently, a TTL policy for the distributed KV cache will aggressively evict less-critical tokens based on attention scores and temporal distance, reducing the state transferred across the network during generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new co-design framework for extreme-scale inference, demonstrating substantial reductions in communication cost and latency compared to state-of-the-art model parallelism. It will deliver open-source system blueprints and a quantitative analysis of the trade-offs between algorithmic fidelity, memory, and communication in distributed LLM serving.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that optimizes model parallelism or KV cache management in isolation, ChronoParallel explicitly couples these two dimensions, using temporal and attention-based metrics from the inference algorithm to directly guide the system's communication and resource scheduling decisions.

proposals.title: WarmBO: Warm-Start Bayesian Optimization for Adaptive and Cost-Efficient LLM Training Scheduling
proposals.motivation: Large-scale LLM training is extremely resource-intensive, with dynamic workloads and hardware heterogeneity making static scheduling suboptimal. While recent work like 'Sleep-time Compute' and 'Continuum' explores efficient resource management for inference, adaptive scheduling for the training loop itself remains a costly, trial-and-error process. A scheduler that can learn operation costs online with minimal initial overhead is crucial for reducing wasted compute and improving training throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, controlled offline profiling phase can generate a strong, transferable prior over operation costs, enabling a Bayesian optimization-based scheduler to achieve stable, near-optimal performance from the first training steps while continuously adapting to runtime variations.
proposals.method: We will design a system that first runs a minimal, representative set of training operations (e.g., forward/backward passes for different model layers and batch sizes) on the target hardware to build a Gaussian Process (GP) prior over latency and memory costs. This prior will warm-start a Bayesian optimization scheduler for the main training job. The online scheduler will use an acquisition function (e.g., Upper Confidence Bound) to select operations, balancing exploitation of the current cost model with exploration to reduce uncertainty, and will update the GP posterior after each observed execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for adaptive, cost-aware scheduling in LLM training, reducing the compute wasted during typical scheduler warm-up periods. It will deliver a concrete implementation and evaluation demonstrating significant reductions in time-to-convergence or total compute cost compared to static or reactive schedulers.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of a lightweight, offline-derived prior into a Bayesian online learning loop for training scheduling, moving beyond inference-time optimizations or heuristic online adapters. This provides a data-efficient way to achieve robust performance from the start of a job, a problem largely unaddressed in pre-2026 LLM systems research.

proposals.title: FusionFlow: Online Cost-Model-Guided Kernel Fusion and Scheduling for Adaptive LLM Training
proposals.motivation: Current compiler-based kernel fusion for LLM training uses static, hardware-agnostic heuristics, which are suboptimal in dynamic, real-world environments like shared clusters or under thermal constraints. As training costs dominate AI progress, optimizing for the actual, observed performance of specific hardware during runtime is critical for maximizing throughput and resource utilization. This gap between static compilation and dynamic execution limits efficiency and portability across diverse and evolving accelerators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight online cost model, which continuously learns the latency and energy costs of fused kernel variants on live hardware, can dynamically generate and schedule more efficient fusion plans than any static strategy, reducing iteration time under variable conditions.
proposals.method: We will build a compiler runtime that instruments the training graph to profile candidate fused and unfused kernel blocks. A Bayesian bandit cost model will be updated with these profiles to learn a hardware response surface. This model will drive a scheduler that re-evaluates fusion strategies at intervals, using a graph partitioning algorithm (e.g., adapted from XLA's operation fusion) but parameterized by real-time cost predictions. The system will be integrated with PyTorch's TorchInductor or JAX's XLA backend to control kernel dispatch.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source system that reduces LLM training iteration time in shared, dynamic clusters by adapting to hardware state. It provides a methodology for co-optimizing kernel fusion and scheduling online, moving beyond offline profiling. The resulting cost model and scheduler will be a step towards truly self-optimizing training frameworks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static fusion in compilers like TVM or XLA, our system continuously learns hardware costs during training and adapts its strategy. It unifies the typically separate concerns of kernel fusion (a compiler pass) and operation scheduling (a runtime task) into a single, feedback-driven optimization loop.

proposals.title: AdaptiveCost: Learning Latency Profiles for Online LLM Scheduling on Heterogeneous Hardware
proposals.motivation: Current LLM inference schedulers, like those managing KV caches, rely on static, pre-profiled cost models for operations (e.g., attention, MLP). This assumption breaks in variable environments like cloud deployments with hardware heterogeneity, shared tenancy, and dynamic throttling, leading to suboptimal throughput and latency. An adaptive system is needed to maintain efficiency as real-time compute and memory costs fluctuate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an online scheduler equipped with a lightweight, continuously updated cost model can learn operation latencies during inference and dynamically optimize scheduling decisions, significantly improving system throughput and tail latency compared to static schedulers in variable hardware environments.
proposals.method: We will extend a scheduler framework like Continuum with a Bayesian bandit learner that treats different scheduling actions (e.g., eviction policies, batch compositions) as arms with stochastic rewards (inverse latency). A small monitoring shim will collect real-time latency observations for core operations. These will update Gaussian Process priors to form a predictive cost model, which the scheduler uses to make adaptive decisions, balancing exploration of uncertain costs with exploitation of known fast paths. The system will be integrated with a time-to-live KV cache manager to co-optimize memory and compute.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, adaptive scheduler for production LLM serving that is robust to hardware variability. It will provide a formal framework for online cost learning in inference systems and demonstrate measurable gains in throughput and latency stability on benchmark workloads across simulated heterogeneous nodes.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that assumes fixed costs (e.g., Sleep-time Compute, Continuum), this system actively learns and adapts to a dynamic cost environment during inference, merging ideas from online learning with core LLM systems optimization.

proposals.title: SketchStream: Hard Memory-Constrained Computation via Streaming Algorithms for LLM Inference and Training
proposals.motivation: The scaling of LLMs is dominated by memory constraints, from the KV cache in long-context inference to the optimizer states in distributed training. Current methods prioritize time efficiency, leading to high, unpredictable peak memory usage that limits deployment on edge devices or in multi-tenant clusters. A principled approach to enforce a strict memory budget is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the design priority to make a hard memory cap the primary constraint, we can adapt streaming algorithms like reservoir sampling and sketching to create new, practical systems for LLM computation that guarantee sublinear memory use with acceptable time/accuracy trade-offs.
proposals.method: We will design a 'Sketching KV Cache' that maintains a bounded, approximate representation of the attention history using techniques like Count-Min Sketch for token importance, enabling infinite-context inference within a fixed memory budget. For training, we will develop a reservoir-sampling-based optimizer that maintains a stochastic sketch of gradient statistics, capping memory for optimizer states. We will prototype these in a system called SketchStream, integrating with PyTorch to evaluate on long-document QA and continual pre-training tasks.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a new family of memory-bounded algorithms for core LLM operations, a system prototype demonstrating their feasibility, and a rigorous analysis of the time-accuracy-memory trade-off frontier. It provides a formal framework for resource-constrained LLM deployment.
proposals.novelty: While streaming algorithms are classic, their application to enforce hard memory caps on LLM internal state (KV cache, optimizer memory) is novel. This fundamentally shifts the system design goal from minimizing latency given enough memory to guaranteeing a memory limit, accepting slower but bounded computation.

proposals.title: JITGraph: Just-in-Time Rematerialization for Dynamic LLM Computation Graphs
proposals.motivation: The increasing deployment of LLMs as agents with complex, multi-turn, and data-dependent control flow creates dynamic computation graphs that defy static memory optimization. Pre-2026 rematerialization strategies are largely static, designed for fixed transformer graphs, leading to inefficient memory usage or high recomputation overhead in dynamic settings. This limits the scale and efficiency of long-running, interactive LLM applications.
