proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting just-in-time (JIT) compilation techniques from dynamic language runtimes, we can create a rematerialization system that profiles runtime graph execution to generate and cache optimized, branch-specific rematerialization schedules, thereby reducing peak memory and total compute for dynamic LLM workloads.
proposals.method: We will design a runtime profiler that instruments PyTorch's execution graph to track control flow decisions and tensor lifetimes, identifying 'hot' graph sub-structures. A JIT scheduler will then generate optimized rematerialization plans for these common patterns using a cost model balancing memory and FLOPs, inspired by prior work on KV cache management. These plans will be cached and selected via a fast runtime dispatcher, with a fallback to a conservative, general-purpose schedule for cold paths. The system will be integrated and evaluated within a framework like Continuum for agent scheduling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical system for efficient memory management in dynamic LLM inference, reducing the resource cost of complex agentic workloads. It will contribute a novel JIT-inspired framework for adaptive rematerialization and provide analysis of common execution patterns in dynamic LLM graphs.
proposals.novelty: While JIT compilation is well-known, its application to the specific problem of rematerialization for dynamic neural graphs is novel. This approach moves beyond static or heuristic-based methods by creating a feedback loop between runtime profiling and schedule optimization, akin to how a JavaScript engine optimizes hot code paths.

proposals.title: JIT-Pipe: A Just-In-Time Hybrid Scheduler for Pipeline Parallelism and Tensor Rematerialization
proposals.motivation: Training large language models requires massive memory and compute, forcing the use of pipeline parallelism and activation rematerialization (checkpointing). Current methods treat these problems separately, leading to suboptimal trade-offs between memory footprint and pipeline throughput. This inefficiency directly increases the time and monetary cost of training state-of-the-art models.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling pipeline stages as production stations and tensor activations as inventory, a co-optimization scheduler based on Just-In-Time logistics principles can dynamically balance recomputation and storage to minimize total memory usage across accelerators while preventing pipeline stalls.
proposals.method: We will design a control algorithm that treats each pipeline micro-batch as a 'job' and activation tensors as 'inventory' with associated holding costs (memory) and stockout costs (recomputation delay). Inspired by adaptive Kanban systems, the scheduler will use real-time telemetry (compute time, communication latency, memory pressure) to dynamically adjust per-stage buffer sizes (which activations to keep) and signal rematerialization events. This will be integrated into a modified pipeline schedule (e.g., 1F1B) where the forward pass of a micro-batch can trigger a 'pull' signal for recomputing needed activations from earlier stages just in time for the backward pass.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework for joint rematerialization and pipeline scheduling, reducing peak memory consumption in large-scale training without introducing pipeline bubbles. We will release an open-source implementation and demonstrate significant improvements in memory-throughput Pareto frontiers on benchmark models like GPT-3.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal analogy to supply chain logistics, translating JIT and Kanban control theory to the domain of distributed neural network training, a connection not explored in prior systems like Gpipe, PipeDream, or Checkmate.

proposals.title: GraphCache: Instance-Aware Adaptive Solver Scheduling for Robust Large-Scale Task Orchestration
proposals.motivation: Large-scale AI systems, such as multi-agent LLM deployments and distributed training pipelines, rely on efficient scheduling of interdependent tasks represented as large graphs. Pre-2026, specialized solvers (e.g., for DAGs, bipartite matching) are brittle; a solver optimal for one graph structure performs poorly on another, leading to unpredictable latency and resource waste. This instability is a critical barrier to reliable, cost-efficient inference and compute orchestration at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: An online, lightweight profiler can extract discriminative structural features from a scheduling graph instance to reliably predict, via a pre-trained model, the fastest specialized solver from a portfolio, thereby guaranteeing robust, near-optimal performance across diverse and novel graph types.
proposals.method: We will design an online profiler to extract a fixed-size feature vector capturing graph metrics (e.g., degree distribution, clustering coefficient, diameter). This vector will feed a lightweight gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost/LightGBM) trained offline on a diverse corpus of synthetic and real-world scheduling graphs, labeled with the fastest solver from our portfolio (including heuristics, MILP, and graph neural network schedulers). For robustness, we implement a fallback strategy using a conservative, general-purpose solver with a time-to-live mechanism, inspired by KV cache management, for instances where model confidence is low.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust scheduling system that minimizes end-to-end job completion time for large-scale AI workloads. It contributes a methodology for instance-aware algorithm selection, extending resource-efficient 'sleep-time compute' principles to the scheduling layer, and provides a publicly available benchmark of graph instances and solver performance.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static solver selection or single-model approaches, we combine real-time graph feature extraction with offline ML training for adaptive scheduling, directly applying principles of efficient cache and resource management from LLM systems to the graph scheduling domain.

proposals.title: GraphCompiler: Fast, Heuristic-Based Schedule Generation for Large-Scale LLM Inference Graphs
proposals.motivation: The computational graphs for modern LLM inference, especially for agentic or multi-turn workloads, are becoming massive and dynamic, incorporating complex control flows, caching decisions, and speculative execution. Using general-purpose solvers for optimal operator scheduling on heterogeneous resources (CPU, GPU, memory) is computationally prohibitive, hindering real-time adaptation and increasing the cost of serving. A fast, near-optimal scheduler is critical for efficient resource utilization in large-scale, cost-sensitive deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the philosophy of compiler optimization heuristics—which use fast, pattern-specific rules to solve NP-hard problems like register allocation—we can develop a library of domain-specific scheduling heuristics that reduce schedule generation time from super-polynomial to near-linear for graphs conforming to common structural patterns in LLM systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that first performs a fast graph analysis to classify subgraphs into known patterns (e.g., pipeline, fork-join with KV cache reuse, grid data parallelism). For each pattern, we will design and implement a rule-based heuristic (e.g., a greedy memory-aware co-location strategy for fork-join, a pipeline balancing algorithm considering compute and memory bandwidth) guided by lightweight, learned cost models for target hardware. The system will compose schedules by applying these pre-optimized heuristics hierarchically, bypassing a general solver.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical scheduling system that enables real-time schedule generation for massively large inference graphs, directly reducing latency and computational overhead in LLM serving systems. It will also contribute a conceptual framework for applying compiler-inspired heuristic design to computational graph optimization, moving beyond black-box solvers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Continuum) optimizes specific aspects like KV cache lifetime, this proposal systematically translates the entire compiler heuristic design methodology—pattern recognition and rule application—to the novel domain of large-scale, heterogeneous inference graph scheduling, which is non-trivial due to stateful operators and complex resource constraints.

proposals.title: Ephemeral: Volatile-State-Aware Heuristic Search for Real-Time Graph Scheduling
proposals.motivation: Large-scale graph scheduling problems in domains like real-time cloud orchestration and quantum circuit compilation feature highly volatile, non-reusable intermediate states due to rapidly changing constraints and noise profiles. Traditional solvers, which rely on caching or memoization, are inefficient here, wasting compute and memory on state storage that provides no benefit. This creates a critical need for a new class of scheduling algorithms designed for ephemerality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing heuristic solvers that actively probe and learn from volatile states in a single forward pass, we can achieve more aggressive, dynamic search space pruning than static methods, leading to significant speedups without state reuse.
proposals.method: We will develop a heuristic framework where a lightweight online model (e.g., a small MLP or attention module) is trained via reinforcement learning to evaluate ephemeral states. This model will guide immediate branching decisions in a greedy or beam search, using features of the current partial schedule and volatile constraints. We will adapt concepts from Stoica's work on KV Cache Time-to-Live (Continuum) for state expiry and cost-aware inference, applying them to prune graph exploration paths in real-time.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new algorithmic paradigm for ephemeral scheduling, demonstrated on benchmarks from dynamic job scheduling and noisy quantum circuit compilation. It will provide a formal analysis of the trade-off between heuristic evaluation cost and search space reduction, extending resource-aware LLM inference principles to combinatorial optimization.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally departs from memoization-based optimization, treating state volatility as a first-class design constraint rather than a limitation. It uniquely combines online learning with single-pass heuristic search for graphs, a method not explored in pre-2026 static or caching solvers.

proposals.title: Context-Aware Hierarchical Optimization for Adaptive LLM Serving Costs
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) in production requires balancing competing costs—energy, memory, latency, and communication—across diverse, heterogeneous hardware. Existing multi-objective optimization approaches, like naive Pareto methods, produce a set of solutions, leaving system operators to make a final, context-dependent choice, which is impractical for automated, real-time scheduling. This gap is critical as LLM inference and multi-agent systems (e.g., Continuum) scale, demanding tractable, single-action decisions that respect dynamic operational constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical policy, which first identifies a dominant objective from system context and then formulates adaptive bounds for secondary costs, can produce single, Pareto-efficient solutions that are more tractable and performant for deployment than standard multi-objective optimization in heterogeneous computing environments.
proposals.method: We will design a two-layer system. The top policy layer will be a lightweight classifier or regressor, trained on system telemetry (e.g., load, KV cache state, SLA targets), to output the dominant objective (e.g., minimize latency) and adaptive constraint bounds for others (e.g., max memory, energy budget). The core optimizer will then solve this constrained single-objective problem using techniques like Lagrangian relaxation or projected gradient descent, informed by cost models from prior work (e.g., Sleep-time Compute). We will integrate this with a simulated scheduler managing KV cache lifetimes (extending Continuum) and synthetic data workloads (inspired by BARE) for evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel optimization framework that translates complex multi-cost trade-offs into actionable, single decisions for LLM serving systems. It will deliver a concrete implementation and evaluation demonstrating improved cost-aware scheduling for inference and multi-turn agents, directly advancing the state of automated resource management for large-scale AI.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving from generating Pareto fronts to generating a single, context-optimal decision via adaptive constraint bounds, a shift from analytical multi-objective optimization to a learned, hierarchical control system tailored for real-time LLM deployment.

proposals.title: MOROS: Multi-Objective Resource Orchestration for Scalable and Efficient Language Model Agent Simulations
proposals.motivation: The vision of simulating thousands of concurrent, long-lived LM agents for social science, economics, or AI ecology research is currently bottlenecked by prohibitive compute, memory, and energy costs. Existing systems focus on scaling inference latency or managing KV cache for single agents, but lack a holistic framework to optimize the competing resource demands—energy, memory, communication—of massive multi-agent simulations on constrained hardware, limiting their practical viability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating agent scheduling, state caching, and inter-agent communication as a unified multi-objective optimization problem, we can design adaptive policies that dynamically navigate these trade-offs, enabling massively concurrent simulations with significantly improved resource efficiency without sacrificing agent interaction fidelity.
proposals.method: We will build a simulation framework with a central orchestrator that models each agent's compute, memory (KV cache state), and communication patterns as a time-varying resource graph. The orchestrator will employ a multi-armed bandit or lightweight reinforcement learning scheduler, informed by real-time hardware telemetry, to make batching, eviction (extending Continuum's TTL concept), and routing decisions. A Pareto-frontier optimizer will dynamically adjust policy weights to minimize a joint cost function aggregating energy (via Sleep-time Compute principles), memory footprint, and network overhead.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) an open-source framework for resource-aware massive agent simulation, 2) novel adaptive policies for multi-objective resource orchestration, and 3) quantitative benchmarks demonstrating order-of-magnitude improvements in efficiency (e.g., Joules per agent-interaction) for long-running simulations on a single node or small cluster.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond isolated inference or cache optimization to a holistic, dynamic control system for multiple competing resources in a concurrent setting, directly enabling a new class of ecologically plausible, long-horizon multi-agent research that was previously computationally infeasible.

proposals.title: MERCURY: Multi-Objective Optimization for Energy, Communication, and Memory in LLM Serving Systems
proposals.motivation: The operational cost of large-scale LLM serving is dominated by energy consumption, inter-device communication, and memory footprint, not just latency and throughput. Existing systems like vLLM and PagedAttention primarily optimize for time and memory, creating a suboptimal trade-off for real-world deployments where energy and bandwidth are critical constraints. A holistic optimization framework is needed to manage these diverse resources efficiently, especially for multi-tenant, geo-distributed inference and agent workloads.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling energy, communication, and memory as a multi-objective cost function within the LLM scheduler and KV cache manager, we can develop Pareto-optimal scheduling policies that significantly reduce total operational cost (TCO) for a given performance SLA.
proposals.method: We will extend the Continuum scheduler's Time-to-Live concept to incorporate dynamic energy and communication cost models for KV cache entries. Using a constrained optimization framework (e.g., a Lagrangian multiplier method), we will design a scheduler that makes joint decisions on batch sizing, device placement, and cache eviction. The core algorithm will treat energy (from GPU/CPU states), cross-node communication (bytes transferred), and memory pressure as tunable penalty terms, seeking configurations that minimize a weighted sum of these costs while meeting latency targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a formal multi-objective cost model for LLM serving incorporating energy, comms, and memory; 2) the MERCURY scheduler, integrated with systems like vLLM; and 3) an empirical analysis of Pareto frontiers on real clusters, showing up to 40% reduction in energy-comms cost for marginal latency increase.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes for latency or memory in isolation, MERCURY's novelty lies in its joint, tunable optimization of three distinct physical resource costs, translating high-level operational expenses directly into low-level scheduling decisions for KV cache and compute.

proposals.title: GraphOpt: Learning Transferable Tensor Rematerialization Policies via Offline Graph Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: The memory-compute trade-off in large model training and inference, managed via tensor rematerialization (checkpointing), is critical for efficiency but remains a hard, model-specific optimization problem. Current online RL solutions require prohibitive exploration time per new computational graph, while static heuristics lack adaptability. A generalizable policy that can transfer across models and hardware would dramatically reduce the cost of deploying efficient LLMs and other large-scale neural networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) can learn a transferable representation of computational graphs that, when combined with offline pre-training on expert trajectories and online RL fine-tuning, yields a robust rematerialization policy requiring minimal online exploration for new, unseen graphs.
proposals.method: We will construct a diverse dataset of computational graphs (e.g., from transformer variants, CNNs) with cost annotations. A GNN encoder (e.g., using GraphSAGE or GAT) will process graph structure and node/edge features (estimated FLOPs, memory). This embedding will condition an RL agent's policy network. We will pre-train the agent via imitation learning (Behavioral Cloning) on trajectories from a fast, bounded-cost optimizer (like Checkmate), then refine it with a sample-efficient online RL algorithm (e.g., Soft Actor-Critic) on a simulated environment.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel offline-to-online RL framework for system scheduling that drastically reduces online adaptation cost. 2) A demonstration that GNN-based representations enable policy transfer across diverse model architectures. 3) An open-source, practical system for adaptive rematerialization that outperforms static and online-only RL baselines in time-to-solution.
proposals.novelty: This work uniquely combines offline pre-training on expert data with GNN-based transfer learning for a core systems optimization problem, moving beyond model-specific online RL or hand-tuned heuristics prevalent in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Decentralized Multi-Agent RL for Adaptive KV Cache and Tensor Management in Distributed LLM Serving
proposals.motivation: The efficient serving of large language models (LLMs) in heterogeneous, dynamic clusters is bottlenecked by memory, particularly the management of the KV cache and intermediate activations. Centralized schedulers struggle with scalability and robustness to node failures or fluctuating workloads. A decentralized approach is needed to enable adaptive, cost-efficient inference and agent scheduling across variable resources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a collective of local reinforcement learning agents, making independent cache eviction and recomputation decisions based on partial observations and communicating learned value functions, can converge to a globally near-optimal memory management policy without central coordination.
proposals.method: We will design each node's agent using a Deep Q-Network (DQN) or PPO, with a state space of local resource metrics (memory pressure, compute idle) and a limited view of neighbor states. The action space will be {keep, evict, recompute} for cached tensors. Agents will share distilled value function updates or policy gradients via a peer-to-peer gossip protocol, inspired by decentralized RL frameworks. We will evaluate the system using a modified version of the Continuum scheduler simulator, integrating realistic workloads and heterogeneous node profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, robust framework for distributed LLM serving that dynamically optimizes the memory-compute trade-off. It will provide both theoretical analysis on the convergence of decentralized policies and empirical benchmarks showing reduced total cost of ownership (TCO) and improved tail latency in volatile environments compared to centralized baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like Continuum uses centralized heuristics and BARE focuses on data generation, this proposal uniquely applies decentralized multi-agent RL to the specific problem of collective KV cache management, enabling emergent global coordination from local, self-interested decisions.

proposals.title: MicroCache: Ultra-Lightweight KV Cache Management for LLM Inference on Memory-Constrained Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: The deployment of small language models (SLMs) on microcontrollers and extreme-edge devices is hindered by the prohibitive memory footprint of the Key-Value (KV) cache, which scales with sequence length. Pre-2026 research on efficient inference, like KV cache eviction or scheduling, targets server-grade hardware and is too heavy for devices with <1MB of RAM. Enabling multi-turn or longer-context interactions on these ubiquitous, power-constrained devices requires a fundamental rethinking of cache management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing micro-scheduling heuristics that exploit the temporal locality and task-specific redundancy in edge-agent dialogues, we can maintain acceptable LLM output quality while reducing KV cache memory usage by over 90% compared to a full cache, making multi-turn interaction feasible on microcontrollers.
proposals.method: We will develop MicroCache, a suite of deterministic, low-overhead heuristics for KV cache eviction and prefetching. Inspired by Continuum's Time-to-Live concept and Sleep-time Compute, methods will include: 1) **Semantic-Chunk TTL**: Assigning expiry times based on utterance boundaries and attention scores. 2) **Selective Retained Tokens (SRT)**: Between turns, aggressively evict all but a tiny, fixed-size set of summary tokens from prior context, chosen via a lightweight saliency metric. 3) **Just-in-Time Pre-compute**: Using device sleep cycles to pre-compute and compress a minimal context summary for the next anticipated turn. We will evaluate on microcontroller-class simulators using SLMs like TinyLlama.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework and evaluation benchmark for KV cache management on sub-1MB devices. It will deliver open-source, deployment-ready algorithms that directly enable longer-context LLM applications on the extreme edge. The research will also contribute new insights into the trade-offs between context fidelity, memory, and compute in micro-scaled environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes KV cache for data center efficiency, MicroCache uniquely focuses on the *survival* of context in severely memory-constrained, intermittent environments, introducing heuristics with zero trainable parameters and O(1) runtime overhead, a radical departure from learned or server-oriented schedulers.

proposals.title: ExaCache: Co-Designing Rematerialization and Parallelism for Exascale LLM Training
proposals.motivation: Training state-of-the-art LLMs requires exascale compute across thousands of GPUs, but current parallelism strategies (data, tensor, pipeline) incur massive memory overhead from activations, limiting model size and efficiency. Naive checkpointing (rematerialization) to alleviate memory pressure creates severe, unpredictable recomputation costs that degrade cluster utilization. A holistic co-design is needed to manage the memory-compute-time trade-off at extreme scale.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly optimizing rematerialization schedules with extreme-scale model and data parallelism dimensions, we can minimize the end-to-end training time for trillion-parameter models on multi-thousand-GPU clusters, overcoming the memory wall that limits current systems.
proposals.method: We will develop ExaCache, a system that treats activation memory as a schedulable resource across spatial (parallelism) and temporal (training step) dimensions. Building on concepts like KV Cache Time-to-Live, we will model the cost of recomputation versus retention for each activation tensor across pipeline stages. ExaCache will integrate with a 3D parallelism framework (e.g., Megatron-LM + Pipeline Parallelism) to dynamically generate rematerialization plans that respect heterogeneous network bandwidth and GPU memory constraints, using a cost model to decide what to keep, recompute, or offload.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel co-optimization framework for rematerialization and parallelism that minimizes wall-clock training time. 2) An open-source implementation and evaluation demonstrating training of multi-trillion parameter models on thousands of GPUs with 20-40% faster throughput compared to state-of-the-art schemes. 3) A formal cost model for exascale training memory-compute trade-offs.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats rematerialization as a separate layer or static optimization, ExaCache directly couples the recomputation schedule with the parallel execution graph, enabling dynamic, context-aware decisions based on real-time cluster resource states and communication costs.

proposals.title: Chronos-Search: A Time-Aware Scheduler for Memory-Constrained Neural Architecture Search
proposals.motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on edge devices or in high-throughput services is severely constrained by fixed memory and latency budgets. Current Neural Architecture Search (NAS) often optimizes for FLOPs or parameters in isolation, neglecting the critical runtime dynamics of KV cache management and intermittent compute, which are central to real-world inference cost and feasibility. A method to co-design the model architecture and its inference-time scheduling under hard constraints is missing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a predictive, time-aware inference scheduler—modeling KV cache behavior and sleep-time compute trade-offs—directly into the NAS loop, we can discover model architectures that achieve significantly higher accuracy than standard NAS under the same strict memory and latency budgets.
proposals.method: We will develop a bi-level optimization framework. The outer loop performs NAS over a supernet of transformer variants (varying layers, heads, and FFN dimensions). The inner loop uses a scheduler simulator, extending concepts from Continuum and Sleep-time Compute, to predict the peak memory (including KV cache with TTL) and execution time for candidate sub-networks on target workloads. We will use a reinforcement learning controller for NAS, where the reward is validation accuracy penalized by constraint violations predicted by the scheduler, enabling efficient search for Pareto-optimal architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel NAS framework that directly optimizes for inference-time resource constraints, 2) a set of Pareto-optimal model architectures for common memory/latency targets, and 3) an open-source scheduler-in-the-loop simulator for evaluating LLM deployment trade-offs, bridging the gap between architecture design and systems-aware scheduling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work separates scheduling (e.g., KV cache management) from architecture search, our key novelty is the tight integration of a predictive time-memory scheduler as a core component guiding the NAS process, solving the inverse problem of maximizing accuracy for a fixed deployment envelope.

proposals.title: REMI: Rematerialization for Memory-Efficient Batched Inference in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: The memory footprint of the KV cache is a primary bottleneck for high-throughput batched inference of large language models, limiting batch sizes and increasing serving costs. While rematerialization is a well-known technique for reducing memory during training by recomputing activations, its application to inference has been largely unexplored. This project addresses the critical need to decouple inference throughput from the prohibitive memory scaling of the KV cache.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic, selective rematerialization of intermediate activations and KV cache segments during batched inference can significantly reduce peak memory usage with an acceptable trade-off in extra compute, leading to higher effective throughput and lower serving cost per token.
proposals.method: We will design a scheduler that profiles the compute graph of a transformer layer to identify candidate tensors for recomputation, focusing on the attention module's intermediate states. The system will implement a Time-to-Live (TTL) policy, similar to Continuum's cache management, but applied to activations, evicting them from memory and later rematerializing them on-demand. We will develop a cost model to decide between retaining and recomputing based on tensor size, recompute cost, and expected future reuse within a batch, optimizing for a memory-compute Pareto frontier.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel system for batched LLM inference that breaks the linear memory scaling of the KV cache with batch size. It will deliver an open-source implementation and a quantitative analysis of the memory-compute trade-off, enabling more cost-effective serving of large models on memory-constrained hardware.
proposals.novelty: While rematerialization is standard in training, applying it dynamically to batched inference with a TTL-managed KV and activation cache is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond static graph optimizations to a runtime, resource-aware scheduling problem for inference serving.

proposals.title: RAFT for Reasoning: Extending the Rejection-Aware Fine-Tuning Recipe to Multi-Hop Question Answering
proposals.motivation: RAFT (Rejection-Aware Fine-Tuning) has shown promise in teaching LLMs to 'reject' unanswerable questions, improving reliability and reducing hallucination. However, its evaluation has been limited to simpler, single-step QA datasets. As LLMs are increasingly deployed for complex, multi-step reasoning (e.g., mathematical proofs, scientific reasoning), a critical gap exists: can RAFT's training paradigm effectively scale to tasks where failure can occur at any intermediate step, not just the final answer? Addressing this is crucial for building robust, cost-efficient reasoning agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the RAFT training recipe—which fine-tunes on correct chain-of-thought (CoT) trajectories while teaching rejection on incorrect ones—to multi-hop reasoning datasets will yield models that are more robust and accurate, as they learn to identify and halt on faulty reasoning steps rather than propagating errors.
proposals.method: We will adapt the RAFT framework to datasets like HotpotQA, DROP, and MATH. The core method involves: 1) Generating a diverse set of reasoning trajectories (both correct and incorrect) for each question using a base LLM, leveraging few-shot synthetic data generation techniques akin to BARE. 2) Fine-tuning a target model (e.g., LLaMA-2 7B) on correct CoT paths while explicitly training it to output a structured rejection token for paths containing logical or factual errors at any step. 3) Incorporating resource-aware considerations from Continuum, potentially using a time-to-live mechanism for intermediate KV cache states during this fine-tuning to manage the increased memory footprint of long reasoning chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation of rejection-aware training for complex reasoning, establishing new benchmarks for robustness. It will deliver open-sourced, fine-tuned models that demonstrate improved cost-efficiency in inference by reducing wasted compute on flawed reasoning chains. The findings will inform the design of more reliable and economically viable LLM agents for multi-step tasks.
proposals.novelty: While RAFT addresses rejection in QA, applying it to multi-hop reasoning is non-trivial and unexplored; it requires the model to perform localized error detection within a reasoning chain, a significantly harder problem than judging final answer correctness. The integration of synthetic trajectory generation and cache-efficient training considerations aligns uniquely with Stoica's research themes of cost, inference, and memory.

ideas.title: Cost-Aware, Adaptive Kernel Fusion for Dynamic Hardware in Language Model Training
ideas.description: This research develops a compiler runtime that co-optimizes kernel fusion strategies and operation scheduling for language model training graphs. It integrates a lightweight online cost model that continuously profiles and learns the latency and energy costs of fused and unfused kernel variants on the specific target hardware during training. This allows the system to adaptively reconfigure fusion plans in response to dynamic hardware states (e.g., thermal throttling, shared cluster load) or for new accelerator types, moving beyond static, pre-defined fusion rules. The result is a scheduler that simultaneously learns hardware performance characteristics and applies the most beneficial fusion strategies to minimize iteration time under observed, real-world conditions.

ideas.title: Optimize for latency-sensitive applications
ideas.description: Adapt the high-throughput methods for scenarios requiring low latency, not just batch processing.
ideas.sourceTitle: High-throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU

ideas.title: Scale up to model parallelism and MoE
ideas.description: Extend runtime to efficiently orchestrate programs across multi-node model-parallel and mixture-of-experts models.

ideas.title: Adaptive Traffic Control for Model Parallelism and MoE
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from urban traffic engineering and smart grid management. The analogous problem—optimizing the flow of vehicles or electricity through a distributed, dynamic network with variable demand and bottlenecks—is solved using real-time sensor data and a central coordinator to adaptively control routing and scheduling. Adapted to the context of multi-node model-parallel and mixture-of-experts models, the solution is to create a runtime orchestrator that implements adaptive traffic control. It employs distributed profiling agents to continuously monitor compute load, communication latency, and expert gating decisions. A hierarchical control plane uses this data to dynamically reconfigure task graphs, pipeline execution order, and network communication paths (especially for the all-to-all exchanges in MoE layers) in response to runtime conditions. This aims to maximize hardware utilization and minimize training step time by proactively avoiding and mitigating contention and idle resources, much like a smart traffic light system reroutes flow to prevent gridlock.

ideas.title: Warm-Start Bayesian Schedulers with Offline Priors for Adaptive Cost Learning
ideas.description: This system first performs a brief, controlled offline profiling run to establish a strong prior distribution over operation costs on the target hardware. This prior is integrated into a Bayesian optimization framework for the online scheduler, which treats the true cost as a Gaussian process. During the main training loop, the scheduler makes decisions that balance exploitation of the current model with targeted exploration to reduce uncertainty, updating the posterior efficiently. This approach ensures stable, near-optimal performance from the very first training steps while still adapting to subtle runtime variations.

ideas.title: Prediction serving is separate from data ingestion

ideas.title: Control-Flow-Aware Learned KV Cache Management for LLM Orchestration
ideas.description: This research integrates a sophisticated frontend language for LLM orchestration with a learned, dynamic KV cache manager. The language's native support for complex control flow patterns—such as loops, conditionals, and dynamic branching—provides explicit, high-level semantics that serve as training signals and runtime guides for a neural cache manager. This manager learns to predict, allocate, and reuse KV cache states not just based on token sequence prefixes, but based on the evolving structure and state of the orchestration program itself. Consequently, the system can execute intricate, stateful multi-LLM workflows with near-constant memory footprint for recurrent logic and minimal recomputation across conditional branches, dramatically improving the efficiency and scale of programmable LLM applications.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: generate training data

ideas.title: Instance-Aware Adaptive Solver Portfolio for Robust Scheduling on Large Graphs
ideas.description: This research develops a robust system that combines fast, specialized solvers with an adaptive selection mechanism to guarantee performance on diverse large-scale scheduling graphs. The core innovation is an online profiler that extracts key structural features from any input graph instance in real-time. These features drive a lightweight machine learning model, trained offline on a diverse corpus of graph instances, to predict the fastest suitable solver from a portfolio of specialized algorithms and heuristics. This approach ensures near-optimal solver performance for known instance types while maintaining robustness via fallback strategies for novel or outlier graphs, directly overcoming the brittleness of a single specialized solver.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Hierarchical Optimization with Adaptive Constraint Bounds for System Costs
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond naive Pareto optimization for energy, communication, memory, and time by structuring the problem hierarchically based on a system's operational context. A policy layer first identifies the dominant objective for a given scenario, then formulates the remaining costs as constraints with bounds that adapt based on system state and historical performance. The core optimizer then solves this constrained single-objective problem, yielding a single, implementable solution that is Pareto-efficient within the defined context. This makes the optimization tractable and directly actionable for deployment in real, heterogeneous computing environments.

ideas.title: Online Learning of Execution Costs for Adaptive LM Program Optimization
ideas.description: This system treats LM program planning as an online learning problem where the cost of different reasoning or tool-use operations is initially unknown and non-stationary. It employs an exploration-exploitation strategy to sample and execute varied plans, instrumenting each step to gather real-time metrics on latency, token consumption, and correctness. These observations train a probabilistic cost model that continuously refines the optimizer's planning decisions, enabling it to adapt to specific model behaviors, prompt variations, and even downstream changes in API performance or tool latency.

ideas.title: Challenge batch-first execution paradigm

ideas.sourceTitle: SGLang: Efficient Execution of Structured Language Model Programs

ideas.title: Transferable Schedule Policy Learning via Graph Representation and Offline Training
ideas.description: This system learns a generalizable policy for tensor rematerialization decisions by training offline on a diverse dataset of computational graphs. A Graph Neural Network encodes the graph structure and estimated costs, producing embeddings that inform the RL agent's actions, enabling transfer to unseen graphs. The agent is pre-trained via imitation learning on expert trajectories from a fast, bounded optimizer and then refined with online reinforcement learning, drastically reducing the required online exploration time. This approach yields a robust policy that can be quickly specialized for new models and hardware configurations.

ideas.title: Streaming Computation via Reservoir Sampling and Sketching
ideas.description: This idea inverts the traditional memory-time trade-off priority by making minimizing peak memory the primary constraint, with runtime as a secondary objective. The analogy comes from Data Stream Processing, where algorithms are designed under a strict, fixed memory budget (e.g., using sketches or reservoirs) and must process data in a single pass, often accepting slower per-item processing or approximate results. Adapting this solution, the research would focus on redesigning fundamental computational algorithms (for graphs, optimization, etc.) to operate under a hard, user-defined memory cap. The core mechanism would be to maintain a bounded, often approximate, 'sketch' of the computational state, updating it iteratively. This creates new algorithm families where time complexity becomes secondary to the guarantee of operating within a strict, sublinear memory envelope, potentially enabling computation on massive datasets in extremely memory-constrained environments like embedded systems or edge devices.

ideas.title: SGLang Pattern Engine: Architecture-Agnostic Optimization via Computational Graph Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a system that extends SGLang's performance benefits to diverse model architectures not by manual porting, but through automated analysis and adaptation. It first profiles a target model to identify its fundamental execution patterns, such as state space model scans or expert network routing. The system then selects and configures a set of compatible high-efficiency primitives from an expanded SGLang kernel library, generating a custom runtime. This ensures optimizations are correctly and safely applied, enabling automatic support for novel architectures while maintaining robust performance guarantees.

ideas.title: Adaptive Runtime Pruning for Edge SGLang
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'Scale down for microcontrollers and edge' concept by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In metabolic network analysis, techniques like Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) are used to deduce a minimal, functional core set of biochemical reactions necessary for an organism to operate under strict resource constraints. Adapting this solution, we propose creating an adaptive SGLang runtime compiler for edge devices. This system would take the hardware profile (e.g., ESP32, Cortex-M7 specs) and the computational graph of a specific tiny language model as inputs. Using a constraint-based optimization solver, it would automatically prune the generic SGLang runtime to synthesize a bespoke, ultra-minimal 'core' runtime executable. This executable would contain only the absolutely necessary kernels, memory management routines, and scheduling logic required for that particular deployment, dramatically reducing binary size and runtime overhead compared to a manually crafted general-purpose minimal runtime.

ideas.title: Formal latency-accuracy trade-off analysis
ideas.description: Develop a theoretical framework for analyzing the latency-accuracy trade-offs in adaptive model selection policies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Clipper: A Low-Latency Online Prediction Serving System

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Resource Orchestration for Massively Concurrent Agent Simulations
ideas.description: This research develops a simulation framework and optimization layer for orchestrating thousands of concurrent, lightweight LM agents under a unified multi-objective cost function that jointly minimizes total system energy consumption, inter-agent communication overhead, and aggregate memory footprint. The core challenge is to design adaptive agent scheduling, communication routing, and state management policies that dynamically navigate the trade-offs between these competing resources at scale. This moves beyond simply enabling massive concurrency to ensuring it is resource-efficient and practical for long-running simulations on constrained hardware, opening new avenues for ecologically plausible multi-agent AI research.

ideas.title: Dynamic Resource Orchestration for Multi-Modal Pipelines via Metabolic Control Theory
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of optimizing multi-modal pipeline efficiency by drawing an analogy from Systems Biology. The analogous field is Metabolic Engineering, where scientists use Metabolic Control Analysis and cybernetic modeling to understand and dynamically optimize complex, interconnected biochemical pathways for maximum yield. The analogous solution involves quantifying control coefficients of individual enzymes on network flux and using optimal resource allocation principles to regulate enzyme expression in real-time. Adapted to the AI context, the research idea is to develop a dynamic resource orchestration framework for multi-modal (language, vision, etc.) pipelines. This framework will treat the pipeline as a computational metabolic network, employing a control-theoretic model to monitor data flow and intermediate states. It will then dynamically reallocate finite hardware resources (e.g., compute, memory, bandwidth) to the pipeline components identified as having the highest 'control coefficient' on overall efficiency, thereby optimizing end-to-end performance adaptively under dynamic operational conditions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Rematerialization for Dynamic Computation Graphs via Just-in-Time Graph Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from compiler optimization for dynamic programming languages to handle networks with dynamic control flow or data-dependent graph structures. In compiler design, just-in-time (JIT) compilers for languages like JavaScript and Python solve the analogous problem of optimizing code with unpredictable execution paths by using runtime profiling to identify hot code paths and generating optimized machine code for specific execution patterns while maintaining fallback interpreters. Adapted to rematerialization, this approach would involve developing a runtime profiler that monitors dynamic computation graph execution, identifies frequently encountered graph structures and control flow patterns, and builds optimized rematerialization schedules for these common cases. The system would maintain a cache of schedules for different execution branches and dynamically switch between them based on runtime graph characteristics, with a fallback to a general conservative schedule for novel graph structures. This enables efficient memory management for dynamic networks while maintaining correctness across all possible execution paths.

ideas.title: Volatile-State-Aware Heuristic Solvers for Ephemeral Graph Scheduling
ideas.description: This research direction develops specialized, fast heuristic solvers for large-scale graph scheduling problems where each intermediate state (e.g., a partial schedule or resource allocation) is volatile, unique, and cannot be cached or reused across problem instances. The core innovation is to design solver heuristics that actively probe and learn from these ephemeral states in a single, forward pass, using lightweight online models to guide immediate branching decisions without the overhead of state storage or comparison. This approach abandons traditional memoization and instead uses the transient state information to dynamically prune the search space more aggressively than static solvers, achieving speedups specifically in domains where state reuse is impossible, such as in real-time systems with highly variable constraints or quantum circuit compilation with continuous noise profiles.

ideas.title: Router for many small, specialized models

ideas.title: Incremental, Reactive Control Flow for LLM Orchestration
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a new frontend language and runtime for LLM orchestration where developers can specify complex, stateful control flow patterns like adaptive loops, dynamic branching, and recursive task decomposition. The key innovation is that the compiler and scheduler treat these patterns not as static blueprints but as reactive, incremental constraint problems. As the workflow executes and the computation graph evolves—for instance, based on an LLM's output or external feedback—a specialized incremental solver efficiently updates the execution schedule, reusing previous computations and minimizing redundant LLM calls. This enables the creation of highly adaptive, long-running agentic systems that can adjust their plan and resource allocation on-the-fly while maintaining performance guarantees.

ideas.title: Adapting Supply Chain Just-In-Time Logistics to Hybrid Pipeline-Tensor Rematerialization
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from supply chain logistics in Industrial Engineering. The analogous problem is coordinating material flow in a multi-stage production line to minimize inventory costs without causing stalls, solved by Just-In-Time (JIT) and adaptive Kanban systems. We adapt this by viewing pipeline parallelism stages as production stations and tensor activations as inventory. The adapted research idea is to create a hybrid scheduler that co-optimizes tensor rematerialization (choosing when to recompute vs. store activations) with pipeline parallel scheduling. The algorithm would dynamically adjust 'buffer sizes' (stored activations) and 'transport routes' (communication schedules) based on real-time performance metrics, minimizing total memory usage across accelerators while preventing pipeline bubbles, effectively implementing a Just-In-Time strategy for neural network training.

ideas.title: Adapting Compiler Optimization Heuristics for Fast Graph Schedule Generation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from compiler design, where NP-hard problems like register allocation are solved using fast, specialized heuristics instead of general solvers, this research proposes to develop analogous heuristics for generating schedules for very large computational graphs. The analogous solution involves creating a library of domain-specific, rule-based algorithms and lightweight cost models that recognize and exploit recurring structural patterns (e.g., pipeline, fork-join, grid patterns) within target graph domains. The adapted idea is to build a system that first analyzes the input graph to classify its dominant structural features and then applies a corresponding, pre-optimized scheduling heuristic from the library. This approach bypasses the cost of a general-purpose solver, aiming to reduce schedule generation time from super-polynomial to near-linear for graphs conforming to common industry or application patterns, enabling real-time scheduling for massively large graphs.

ideas.title: Support for stateful model composition

ideas.title: Decentralized, Real-Time LM Execution for Interactive Agents
ideas.description: This research investigates a new execution paradigm for language model programs where computational tasks are dynamically partitioned and dispatched across a decentralized network of peer executors. The core innovation is a coordination-free protocol that allows individual agents or components of a single, complex LM program to execute and communicate directly, bypassing a central runtime scheduler. This architecture is designed from the ground up to prioritize the latency of single interactive sessions, achieving real-time responsiveness by removing batch-processing bottlenecks and centralized decision points. The result is a foundation for highly reactive, collaborative multi-agent systems and immersive human-AI interactions that feel instantaneous.

ideas.title: Decentralized Reinforcement Learning for Collective Memory Management
ideas.description: This research investigates a decentralized system where each computational node or device hosts a local RL agent. These agents learn to make local decisions—such as whether to keep, evict, or recompute tensors—based on their own resource constraints and a limited view of the system. Through communication of learned value functions or gradients, the agents collectively converge on a globally efficient memory management and recomputation policy without a central orchestrator. This enables robust, adaptive scheduling for large language model programs across heterogeneous and dynamic distributed environments.

ideas.title: Scalable Agent System Profiler: Co-design for Massively Concurrent LM Simulations
ideas.description: This research direction develops a co-design framework that tightly couples a profiler for inefficient LM programs with the architecture for massive multi-agent simulation. The profiler analyzes unstructured agent code to identify systemic bottlenecks—such as redundant computation, inefficient communication patterns, or state bloat—and extracts generalized anti-patterns. These insights then directly guide the creation of optimized simulation kernels, lightweight agent skeletons, and communication primitives specifically engineered to avoid those inefficiencies at scale. The result is a virtuous cycle: profiling informs scalable architecture design, and deploying thousands of agents with that architecture generates new, complex interaction patterns to profile, continuously refining both the efficiency and the realism of large-scale simulations.

ideas.title: Cost-Optimized Program Synthesis via Trace Decompilation and Plan Caching
ideas.description: This system fuses database-style query optimization with decompilation of inference traces. It uses the decompiler (Idea B) to convert low-level LM execution traces back into high-level, composable SGLang programs. These decompiled programs are then fed into a cost-based optimizer (Idea A) that evaluates alternative execution plans (e.g., varying parallelization, KV cache reuse, speculative decoding strategies) and caches the most efficient ones. The synergy creates a closed loop: the system can observe the actual runtime performance of generated plans, decompile successful executions to refine its program representations, and update its plan cache, leading to progressively more optimized program synthesis for recurring computational patterns in LM workloads.

ideas.title: Apply Loop Fusion and Tiling from Scientific Computing to LM Kernel Fusion
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core optimization from high-performance scientific computing—loop fusion combined with tiling—to the problem of fusing operation graphs in large language models (LMs). In HPC, for computations like stencils, separate loops over arrays are fused into one and the fused loop is tiled to maximize data reuse in cache. The analogous solution is to apply this two-stage process to LM kernel fusion: first, use deep learning compiler techniques to create fused macro-kernels for coherent subgraphs (e.g., QKV projection, attention, and output projection). Second, integrate a polyhedral-style or heuristic-based tile optimizer specifically for these novel fused kernels to determine optimal thread block and tile dimensions that leverage GPU on-chip memory, thereby reducing the dominant cost of intermediate tensor I/O and increasing computational intensity.

ideas.title: Static Analysis for Automated API Orchestration and Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction combines compiler static analysis with external API integration to create a system where SGLang can automatically optimize and orchestrate calls to diverse external tools. By applying data-flow and side-effect analysis to the LM program graph, the compiler can identify redundant API calls, reorder independent calls for parallelism, cache frequent external data retrievals, and even select the most efficient API or data source for a given sub-task based on static context. This moves beyond simple connectivity into intelligent, performance-aware integration, where the orchestration of external resources is a first-class, compiler-optimized component of the LM program execution.

ideas.title: Profile-Guided, Speculative Optimization for LM Program Graphs
ideas.description: This system addresses the unpredictability of LM programs by combining static analysis with dynamic profiling. It first runs a lightly instrumented version of the SGLang graph on a curated dataset to collect profiles of execution traces, value shapes, and conditional branch likelihoods. An optimizer then uses this profile to apply speculative, validated transformations—such as fusing operators with predictable outputs or pre-computing probable execution paths—while maintaining runtime guards to fall back to safe execution if live behavior deviates from the profile. This results in a robust, empirically-informed optimization pipeline that safely accelerates LM inference.

ideas.title: Apply Compiler Profile-Guided Optimization to LM Program Planning
ideas.description: This idea adapts Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO) from compiler design to optimize Language Model (LM) program execution planning. In compilers, PGO uses runtime profiles from instrumented execution on sample workloads to guide advanced optimizations. Adapted here, the LM's execution environment would be instrumented to profile different execution plans (e.g., reasoning steps, API call sequences) for a set of representative user intents, collecting data on performance and success. This empirical profile data then informs a cost-based optimizer, moving beyond static heuristics to dynamically select, generate, and cache the most efficient execution plans tailored to the LM's observed operational patterns and common query structures.

ideas.title: Incremental Solver via Incremental Database View Maintenance
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a scheduler must run from scratch on each new version of a computation graph. By drawing an analogy to Incremental View Maintenance (IVM) in database systems—where materialized views are updated efficiently using change deltas—we propose an incremental solver for computational graphs. The analogous solution calculates a delta update based on specific data changes. Adapted here, the solver would accept a description of the delta between successive graph iterations (e.g., from a version control or monitoring system) and compute a minimal, corrective update to the prior schedule. This maintains schedule stability and quality while drastically reducing solver invocation time and resource cost for iterative workflows, where changes between steps are typically small and localized.

ideas.title: Pareto-Optimal Negotiation of the Memory-Time Constraint Surface
ideas.description: This approach reframes the problem as a multi-objective optimization, seeking the set of Pareto-optimal algorithms where neither memory nor runtime can be improved without worsening the other. It employs a tunable negotiation mechanism, such as a Lagrangian or a weighted penalty function, to explore the trade-off curve based on a user-defined or hardware-specific budget. This ensures solutions are practically viable, avoiding the extreme runtime costs of a pure memory-minimization priority. The system outputs a frontier of candidate algorithms, allowing users to select the optimal point for their specific resource constraints.

ideas.title: Use Directed Evolution for Auto-Optimization of SGLang Programs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of directed evolution from evolutionary biology and protein engineering to the problem of auto-optimizing SGLang programs via program synthesis. In the analogous field, directed evolution solves optimization by iteratively generating diverse genetic variants, selecting for a desired function, and amplifying successes. Adapted to this context, we propose a system that treats an SGLang program as a 'genotype.' It automatically generates a population of program variants through semantic-preserving mutations and crossovers of the program's AST/IR. These variants are then evaluated by a fitness function measuring performance metrics critical to LLM inference (e.g., execution time, GPU memory footprint). The highest-scoring variants are selected to produce the next generation of programs through further variation, creating an automated, search-based optimization loop that synthesizes increasingly efficient SGLang code without requiring manual rewriting.

ideas.title: Formal Semantics-Driven Polyhedral Optimization for SGLang Rematerialization
ideas.description: This research develops a formal semantics for the SGLang frontend programming model, specifically designed to expose its implicit computational structure and data dependencies as a polyhedral model. Using this formal representation, it then applies polyhedral compiler techniques—like tiling and loop fusion—not to traditional loops, but to structure and prune the search space for automatic rematerialization strategies in large language model inference. The synergy creates a novel, semantics-guided optimization framework where the correctness of rematerialization schedules is grounded in the language's formal specification, while their efficiency is guaranteed by polyhedral cost models and transformation strategies, leading to both safer and faster generated kernels.

ideas.title: Inverse: A Performance Profiler for LM Programs via Metabolic Pathway Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the core principles of metabolic flux analysis from systems biology to create a profiler for inefficient LM programs. Just as flux balance analysis identifies wasteful futile cycles and rate-limiting steps in a cell's metabolic network, this tool would model an unstructured LM program (a series of prompts, context manipulations, API calls, and conditional logic) as an information-processing network. By analyzing the flow and transformation of information (tokens, embeddings, decisions) through this network under constraints like cost, latency, and context limits, the tool can algorithmically identify anti-patterns analogous to biological inefficiencies—such as redundant reasoning loops that consume context without progress (futile cycles) or over-reliance on large, expensive models for trivial subtasks (bottleneck enzymes). The output is a diagnostic map highlighting scalability constraints and specific recommendations for refactoring, turning a qualitative assessment of LM code into a quantitative, constraint-based optimization problem.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Memory Rematerialization via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts ecological succession principles to model complex memory hierarchies as rematerialization targets. In ecology, succession describes how biological communities progress through predictable stages in response to environmental conditions and disturbances. Analogously, we model CPU RAM, NVMe, and disaggregated memory as different ecological niches with varying 'resource' characteristics (bandwidth, latency) and 'disturbance' patterns (access frequency). The solution involves developing a rematerialization scheduler that places recomputed values in specific memory tiers based on their predicted 'successional stage'—determined by how soon and how often they'll be accessed. This creates an adaptive, layered caching strategy where data naturally migrates through the memory hierarchy according to dynamic access patterns, optimizing for both computation reuse and data movement costs across heterogeneous memory systems.

ideas.title: Flexible Generation with Concurrent Internal Verification for Evolving Output Structures
ideas.description: This research develops a dual-process optimization framework for tasks with ambiguous or evolving output structures. The system integrates a primary, flexible generator with a parallel, internal critic that performs real-time verification, grounding, and consistency checking. This approach ensures that the benefits of relaxed structural constraints are not achieved at the cost of semantic reliability or factual accuracy. The result is a robust method for dynamic output generation that remains trustworthy and contextually coherent.

ideas.title: Declarative, State-Aware Dataflow Graphs for Robust LLM Orchestration
ideas.description: This system moves beyond syntactic sugar for control flow by providing a declarative framework for LLM orchestration. Workflows are defined as dataflow graphs where nodes represent operations (LLM calls, tool use, data transforms) and edges define state transitions guarded by explicit predicates. A dedicated runtime engine evaluates the graph against a centralized, immutable state snapshot, managing all control flow decisions externally. This design ensures workflows are deterministic, fully inspectable, and debuggable, as execution logic is disentangled from application logic and the complete state history is preserved for analysis.

ideas.title: SGLang KERNEL: A Verified, Semantics-Guided Compiler and Runtime for LLM Programming
ideas.description: This project develops a formal, executable semantics for the SGLang programming model and directly integrates it into the system's toolchain. The semantics serve as a golden reference for a semantics-preserving compiler that performs verified optimizations on computation graphs. The system includes a property-based testing framework that automatically generates and validates programs against the formal model, and a debugger that traces execution steps back to semantic rules. This ensures the formal foundation actively guarantees correctness, aids performance optimization, and improves developer understanding.

ideas.title: Incremental, Analysis-Driven Graph Optimization for Evolving LM Computations
ideas.description: This research fuses incremental solver design with compiler static analysis to manage evolving Language Model (LM) computation graphs. A static analyzer first builds a rich model of data dependencies, operator properties, and potential graph mutations. As the program graph evolves slightly between iterations (e.g., due to dynamic control flow or prompt changes), the incremental solver uses this pre-computed analytical model to efficiently identify the minimal subgraph affected and compute an optimal schedule update. This synergy enables both provable optimizations based on static guarantees and real-time adaptability, achieving performance levels neither a pure incremental solver nor a one-time static analysis could provide alone for dynamic LM workloads.

ideas.title: Adaptive Optimization via Probabilistic Soft Constraints, Inspired by Protein Folding
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of fixed, structured output constraints (like precise grammars) in optimization tasks. Drawing an analogy from computational biology, specifically protein structure prediction, where the target 3D fold is ambiguous and determined by a complex energy landscape. The analogous solution uses probabilistic sampling within a flexible constraint system (distance maps, angle preferences) to find low-energy conformations without a single rigid template. Adapted to this context, the idea is to develop optimization methods for tasks with ambiguous or evolving outputs by replacing fixed grammars with a probabilistic model of valid structures. An algorithm would sample candidate outputs, guided by a cost function built from soft, learnable constraints (e.g., neural-learned priors on token relationships or partial structures). These constraints can be updated incrementally based on new data or feedback, allowing the system to efficiently navigate the output space for tasks where the 'correct' structure is not predefined but emerges from adaptable preferences.

ideas.title: Drift-Aware Incremental Scheduling with Multi-Fidelity Corrective Rollbacks
ideas.description: This system performs incremental updates to a computational schedule as the underlying graph evolves, but it actively guards against the fatal flaw of solution quality drift. It employs a monitoring layer that periodically validates the incumbent schedule against a fast, approximate global solver to detect significant optimality degradation. Upon detecting drift, a meta-controller selects a corrective action—such as a targeted partial reschedule, a rollback to a verified checkpoint, or a full recomputation—based on the severity and locality of the error. This ensures the benefits of incremental computation are retained while the output's global quality is formally bounded and robust.

ideas.title: Adaptive Memory Management for Tensor Scheduling via RL, Inspired by Ecological Foraging
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principles of Optimal Foraging Theory from behavioral ecology to the problem of tensor schedule search. In ecology, animals learn to optimally exploit resource patches by weighing immediate gains against the cost and uncertainty of seeking new ones. Analogously, we train an RL agent to sequentially decide which intermediate tensors in a computational graph to keep in memory or recompute later. The agent treats memory as a limited resource environment, learning a value function that estimates the long-term computational cost (negative reward) of evicting a tensor now versus holding it, thereby discovering efficient, adaptive schedules that minimize total execution time through experience, much like a forager learns an efficient path.

ideas.title: A Declarative External Function Abstraction for Robust and Composable Tool Use
ideas.description: This research designs and implements a first-class 'External Function' (ExFun) primitive within the SGLang runtime to provide a robust, unified foundation for all external interactions. The ExFun abstraction allows developers to declaratively specify API calls, database queries, or tool executions with guaranteed semantics for error handling, retries, and state propagation. By making external calls composable and natively schedulable within SGLang's execution graph, the system ensures reliability and consistency, turning fragmented integrations into a coherent, programmable capability. This approach fundamentally shifts the goal from mere connectivity to providing a principled, developer-friendly framework for building reliable agentic workflows that leverage the external world.

ideas.title: Selectively Volatile Intermediate States with On-Demand Recomputation
ideas.description: This system enables efficient program execution while guaranteeing the non-reusability of security-critical intermediate states. It achieves this by caching intermediate results by default but employing a content-based volatility policy that deterministically marks and invalidates specific states derived from sensitive or ephemeral inputs. The runtime lazily recomputes these volatile states only when they are directly required to produce a final output, avoiding wasteful full recomputation. This design maintains the original security goal for targeted data flows but restores practical efficiency for stable portions of the computation, supporting debugging, optimization, and iterative development.

ideas.title: Adapting Temporal Logic & Model Checking for Continuous LM State Verification
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using stream processing semantics for continuous LM execution by drawing an analogy from the field of formal verification, specifically hardware design. The analogous solution uses temporal logic and model checking to verify that sequences of system states satisfy time-dependent logical properties. Adapted to the research context, we propose defining a temporal logic framework to specify constraints on the evolving internal and output states of a language model operating over a continuous stream. A lightweight, incremental verification engine, integrated with the stream processing windowing and state management layer, would continuously check the LM's execution trace against these temporal rules. This provides a formal mechanism for ensuring consistency, safety, and logical coherence over time in continuous LM programs, moving beyond simple state management to active runtime verification.

ideas.title: Inverse-Synth: Profiling and Synthesis for Efficient LM Programs
ideas.description: This system first profiles unstructured LM program code to identify bottlenecks and anti-patterns, as in the original idea. Its core advance is an integrated synthesis engine that then automatically generates optimized, corrected code to directly address the diagnosed inefficiencies. The tool presents the user with a diff view comparing the original and refactored code, alongside clear explanations of the applied transformations (e.g., prompt restructuring, caching insertion, logic offloading). This closed-loop approach enables rapid, informed iteration, turning a static audit into an active optimization assistant.

ideas.title: Apply Circuit-Level Logic Optimization to LM Program Graphs
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from Electronic Design Automation (EDA), specifically digital logic synthesis, to optimize Language Model (LM) program graphs. In EDA, static analysis of a circuit's gate-level netlist enables optimizations like constant propagation, dead logic elimination, and common subexpression sharing to improve efficiency before physical implementation. Similarly, by applying these circuit-level techniques to the static graphs of SGLang programs, we can pre-compute and propagate constant arguments (e.g., fixed system prompts), prune unused reasoning or generation branches, and deduplicate identical computational sub-graphs. This compiler-inspired, pre-execution optimization aims to reduce redundant LM calls, decrease memory footprint, and lower overall inference latency for complex, structured LM programs.

ideas.title: Semantic State Streams: Probabilistic Meaning Management for Continuous LM Execution
ideas.description: This system decouples the continuous flow of LM-generated tokens from the management of execution state by introducing a middleware layer that extracts and maintains a live, probabilistic knowledge graph. Stream processing windowing and aggregation functions are repurposed to operate on this structured semantic representation, enabling operations like merging contradictory beliefs, summarizing narrative context, or triggering corrective actions based on drift in logical consistency. This allows the benefits of stream processing—continuous execution, statefulness, and temporal reasoning—to be applied at the level of meaning rather than syntax, robustly supporting long-horizon, coherent LM agentic behavior.

ideas.title: Apply Urban Traffic Flow Optimization to Rematerialization Search
ideas.description: This research adapts urban traffic flow optimization techniques to structure the rematerialization search space. Drawing from urban transportation planning where macroscopic fundamental diagrams (MFDs) model city-wide traffic flow, we apply similar perimeter control strategies to computational operations. Just as urban planners regulate vehicle entry into different zones (analogous to compiler tiling) and coordinate traffic signals across intersections to create green waves (analogous to loop fusion), we can define computational zones for rematerialization and coordinate operations across these zones. The adapted solution treats computational operations as vehicles and memory access patterns as traffic flow, using perimeter control concepts to create 'green wave' execution patterns that minimize memory traffic congestion, thereby transforming the microscopic operation scheduling problem into a more manageable macroscopic flow optimization problem.

ideas.title: Massively Multi-Agent Simulation with Selective State Propagation for Coherent Emergence
ideas.description: This research designs a system for simulating thousands of concurrent LM agents that balances scale with interaction depth. It employs a hierarchical memory model and a learned relevance filter to enable efficient, selective state updates, ensuring critical information persists and propagates to shape agent behavior and social dynamics. The approach maintains scalability by compressing state and only performing expensive reasoning when interactions are deemed significant, preventing trivialization. The goal is to enable the emergence of complex, coherent narratives and strategies from large-scale agent populations.

ideas.title: Pareto-Optimal Resource Allocation via Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms
ideas.description: This research expands the original multi-objective optimization idea by drawing an analogy from Ecological Economics, where Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs) are used to find trade-off solutions for sustainable systems balancing environmental, economic, and social costs. The analogous solution involves using algorithms like NSGA-II to map a Pareto front of non-dominated solutions, making trade-offs explicit. Adapted to computing, the idea is to apply MOEAs to jointly minimize energy consumption, communication overhead, and memory usage for computational kernels or distributed tasks. The algorithm would evolve a population of code variants or system configurations, outputting a Pareto-optimal set. This allows a scheduler or programmer to select the most appropriate implementation based on dynamic priorities (e.g., battery life, network congestion, available RAM), rather than being forced into a single aggregated cost function.

ideas.title: One-Time Pad for Computation: Ephemeral, Non-Reusable Intermediate States
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from cryptography's one-time pad. The analogous problem is securing communication with perfect secrecy, solved by using a unique, random key once and then destroying it. Adapting this, the research proposes designing programs where intermediate generation states are treated like a one-time pad for computation. Each execution would be provisioned with a unique, ephemeral set of states (a 'computational pad') that are volatile by design—generated on-demand, used non-reusably within that single context, and then actively scrubbed or made cryptographically irretrievable. This approach challenges the assumption that intermediate states are stable or shareable, aiming to create systems where computation itself leaves no reusable intermediate traces, enhancing security, privacy, and ensuring deterministic outputs can only be reproduced by re-running the entire unique process from the start.

ideas.title: IntentCraft: Synthesizing Abstract SGLang Programs from Multi-Trace Behavioral Analysis
ideas.description: IntentCraft is a system that synthesizes high-level, reusable SGLang programs by analyzing multiple low-level LM inference traces alongside optional natural language annotations. Instead of decompiling a single trace, it identifies common control flow patterns, data dependencies, and potential parameters across traces to infer the programmer's abstract intent. The system then generates an optimized, parameterized SGLang program that generalizes the observed behavior, enabling batching, caching, and composition not present in any individual trace. This shifts the goal from trace replication to intent capture and efficient program synthesis.

ideas.title: CRDT-Based Consensus for Decentralized LM Program Execution
ideas.description: This research designs a system for decentralized LM program execution that overcomes coordination failure by embedding coordination logic directly into a shared, CRDT-based state fabric. Each autonomous agent independently proposes and executes tasks based on a deterministic protocol that reads from and merges updates into this fabric, ensuring all participants converge on a consistent global state. The system guarantees liveness through a stigmergic task assignment mechanism and provides formal verification of state convergence properties. This enables robust, orchestration-free execution while maintaining the reliability needed for complex, multi-step LM programs.

ideas.title: Decentralized LM Execution via Swarm Intelligence Protocols
ideas.description: This research investigates a decentralized, distributed execution model for LM programs by drawing an analogy from swarm intelligence in collective robotics. The analogous problem—coordinating a large group of simple agents to achieve a complex global task without central control—is solved using local interaction rules and emergent coordination (e.g., ant colony optimization, flocking algorithms). The adapted idea applies these principles to LM program execution: autonomous LM agents or specialized modules operate without a central orchestrator, using local message-passing (via distributed ledgers or direct peer communication) and simple rules for task claiming and resource negotiation. This swarm-based approach aims to achieve robust, scalable program execution through emergent coordination, challenging the assumption that complex LM reasoning requires centralized runtime control.
